column1: Text

When cars really took off in the early 1900s, especially with Ford's Model T, they became incredibly widespread. Now, lots of folks are asking if we maybe use cars too much, and you know what? I kind of agree it's time to ease up on all the driving.

Elizabeth Rosenthal's piece, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," shines a light on a major issue. Experts are saying that cars, which you see everywhere in suburbs just like in Shanghai or Chicago, make it a real struggle to cut down on vehicle pollution. Cars cause 12% of Europe's pollution, but get this – it's a huge 50% in the US because we depend on them so heavily! And check out Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog." It really hammers home just how awful cars are for the planet. Paris had to ban cars based on license plates just to tackle the smog! Seriously!

Then we have Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," which is awesome. Millions in Colombia left their cars at home for a day to walk, bike, or hop on the bus. The streets were suddenly so quiet and chill. This yearly thing bans private cars, but buses and taxis can still zip around the city of seven million. And it's resulted in cleaner air. The city has been getting a makeover with new parks, sports areas, sidewalks, and businesses popping up everywhere.

So, it looks like cutting back on cars and having car-free days can really do some good, like making the air cleaner and improving our cities. What happened in Colombia shows that it's doable to ease traffic and make things better. Perhaps if we did something similar in America, like biking or walking more for those short trips, we could make things way more sustainable. To be honest, I think cutting back on cars is a fantastic idea.

Across the globe, transportation is fundamental to nations far and wide, offering a means for people to traverse distances more rapidly via automobiles and transit systems. Yet, this reliance carries a burden: widespread pollution. A great deal of us drive regularly, and it contributes significantly to air contamination, demanding that we pursue avenues for emission reduction.

Consider Vauban, a quaint German village. Its inhabitants embrace a car-free lifestyle, favoring residences strategically located near essential amenities like grocery stores. This design enhances accessibility, and while vehicles are not entirely absent, an astounding three-quarters of Vauban's populace forgo car ownership. Astonishingly, over half the families relinquished their vehicles to reside there, reporting decreased stress levels due to diminished driving demands. At present, vehicle emissions account for 12% of global pollution, a figure escalating to 50% in car-dependent regions like many areas in the United States.

Paris confronted severe smog with an innovative solution. On Mondays, vehicles with license plates ending in even numbers faced driving restrictions, enforced by strict penalties. The subsequent day saw a reversal, with odd-numbered plates banned. This initiative yielded an immediate 60% reduction in traffic congestion within a mere five days. Diesel-powered vehicles, prevalent in France, were a major contributor to the smog crisis. However, the effectiveness waned after the alternating ban was discontinued.

Picture a day devoid of cars. This reality materializes annually in Bogota, Colombia, where private vehicles are prohibited, save for taxis and buses. This single day significantly curtails pollution. This tradition dates back to the 1990s, followed by the creation of bike lanes, parks, and community centers. Embracing a car-free existence presents challenges but ultimately benefits the environment.

While complete car elimination seems improbable in the near future, nations should prioritize pollution reduction and encourage decreased car usage. Actions aimed at mitigating air pollution would yield tangible improvements. Despite our reliance on vehicles, their overuse inflicts damage upon our environment and well-being.

Elisabeth Rosenthal wonders if America's love of cars is waning. Her big idea? Car use is going down, which might actually be great news! Could less driving mean fewer accidents, healthier lives, and cleaner air? The thought is that we might not need cars as much.

First, driving less could really improve our health and safety – super important stuff. Andrew Selsky shared how quiet and nice the streets were in Colombia on a car-free day. Less traffic means less stress. Traffic jams mess up our schedules and make us crazy. So, less driving? Less traffic and stress! Heidrun Walter said ditching her car made her less stressed and happier. Plus, we'd get more exercise. Sure, cars are easy, but we're so busy we forget to move. Selsky noticed lots of new parks and sports areas. Fewer cars can mean more parks, and more parks mean more exercise. Basically, less driving equals less stress and better health!

Also, less driving helps the Earth. Maybe not everyone is an environmentalist, but it's a big deal. Pollution harms everything, and cars are big polluters. Rosenthal mentions that less driving "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." Less driving, less pollution – a chance for a brighter future. Pollution is a serious issue and drivers contribute to it.

Additionally, we'd rely less on cars. Many think they NEED them, but are they addicted? Technology has changed us, and we're losing touch with the natural world. Being overly dependent isn't ideal. David Goldberg notes that "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Some may disagree, but relying on something that could harm us isn't progress. Rosenthal explains that "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway." Not everyone can even afford a car. People who depend on cars have forgotten about nature and don't always consider the environment. Less driving could fix that.

In short, driving less could mean better health, less pollution, and less dependence on cars. It's a cleaner, safer world for all. Maybe America is improving by driving less and thinking about what benefits everyone.

Do you drive a lot? Do you use your car for work or to shop? Did you ever think about how many people drive, even if it's not far? It's easy to not think about how driving affects the planet. Can you not drive today? People say, "I need my car!" But, you can drive less. Cities like Havana, Paris, and Bogotá have done it. Driving less is good for you and others.

Funnily enough, less driving can make you happy. Heidrun Walter said people feel less stressed without a car. Think about road rage. It's not fun. Driving takes work – finding your way, dealing with rude drivers – which stresses you out. Big cities like New York and Paris often have traffic and dirty air. Robert Duffer wrote in "Paris bans driving due to smog" that warm air traps car fumes, which is bad. Duffer also said Paris banned cars for a bit because of bad pollution, which made the air clean fast. People can walk, bike, or skate instead, which is good for you and makes you feel better than sitting in a car.

Using cars less makes cities look better. Pollution hurts plants and smells bad, making things gloomy. Less driving means less bad fumes and cleaner cities. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote in "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars" that fewer cars mean more people walk, which means more sidewalks and shops. Andrew Selsky said they added parks, sports areas, nice sidewalks, new restaurants, and cool stores. The EPA likes areas with fewer cars, as Rosenthal said, which cuts down on pollution. Bogotá, Colombia, has a "car-free" day every year, fining drivers. Even though fining people is tricky, it works, reducing smog. So, driving less is good for your health, better cities, and a cleaner world.

Cars, undeniably, possess a certain appeal. Yet, their significance in our lives is perhaps greater than we often acknowledge. Fundamentally, they are vehicles of transport, facilitating our movement from one location to another with remarkable speed. While alternatives such as trains and buses exist, cars are often favored for their perceived superiority. However, the pervasive use of cars contributes to both environmental degradation and personal stress levels. Opting for buses or trains mitigates the need for costly repairs, alleviates traffic congestion, and consequently, reduces pollution. Therefore, a reduction in car usage emerges as a desirable objective.

The advantages of reduced car dependence extend to both human well-being and planetary health, most notably through improvements in air quality. The combustion engines of cars release harmful greenhouse gases into the atmosphere. The impact of a single vehicle may seem negligible. However, the cumulative effect of countless cars, particularly in densely populated urban centers such as Paris, poses a significant environmental challenge. In response to severe pollution levels, Paris has implemented driving restrictions. According to Robert Duffer in "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," vehicles with license plates ending in even numbers were temporarily prohibited, subject to fines for non-compliance, later alternating to odd numbers. This intervention resulted in a 60% reduction in traffic congestion. Similarly, Andrew Selsky reports in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota" that Bogota, Colombia, observes car-free days to promote cleaner air, encourage alternative modes of transportation, and foster environmental sustainability. This global recognition underscores the benefits of curtailing car usage for environmental preservation.

Consider also the reduction in stress! Driving, with its attendant challenges of traffic congestion, mechanical failures, and incessant noise, can be a highly stressful activity. Therefore, reducing driving time inherently leads to a decrease in stress. The absence of traffic jams, repair bills, and auditory distractions cultivates a more tranquil existence. Elisabeth Rosenthal quotes Hartmut Walter in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," stating, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." This sentiment highlights the stress-reducing benefits of car-free living. Echoing this perspective, Andrew Selsky notes in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota" that car-free days present "a good opportunity to take away stress..." Prioritizing peace of mind is a prudent choice, as reduced stress contributes to an improved quality of life.

Ultimately, reducing car dependency yields positive outcomes for both the environment and individual well-being. Our planet stands in dire need of restorative action, and stress exerts a detrimental impact on our health. By embracing strategies that lessen car usage, we can simultaneously address environmental concerns and alleviate personal stress. While the initial transition may present challenges, the widespread adoption of such practices demonstrates its viability and efficacy, ultimately fostering a more sustainable and harmonious existence.

This Electoral College thing is odd, isn't it? Instead of just picking the president ourselves, we actually pick people who then pick for us, whether they're Democrats or Republicans. But guess what? Source two says those electors can actually ignore what we want! How fair is that? I mean, we vote hoping for someone good who will make things better, but this kind of messes that up. You know what's the worst? That "winner-take-all" thing.

We don't get to directly pick the president these days. Nah, as source two points out, we vote for electors. Every state gets a certain amount. These electors then go on to decide who wins. So, get this, source two says there are 538 electors, and you need 270 to win. The number of electors each state gets is based on how many folks they have in Congress. If we want things to be fair, every single vote has to matter! Getting rid of the Electoral College means every vote would truly count. Candidates would have to earn votes by having real support, and ties would be super rare. Right now, if most people in your state want someone else, your vote doesn't matter at all. Get rid of the college, and BAM, your vote counts!

Source two even talks about "faithless" electors, meaning some people might vote for the other person! It's like a slap in the face to the whole country. A Republican elector voting Democrat is like saying all those Republican votes didn't matter. We only get to pick a president every four years. Electors changing their vote? No way.

Sources two and three keep bringing up the "winner-take-all" thing. It's brutal. If you lose, your votes mean nothing. The winner gets everything! It's as if all those people who voted for the loser really wanted the winner all along.

So, senators, get rid of the Electoral College! It's just a bad system. It makes some votes worth more than others. It lets electors vote based on whatever they feel like. And that "winner-take-all" thing? It's like throwing away all the votes for the people who didn't win.

Dear Senator,

I am writing regarding a vital adjustment to how we choose our president. It is paramount that we consider abolishing the Electoral College in favor of a system that more accurately represents the populace's collective will. Currently, this archaic system breeds inequity - electors possess the autonomy to contravene the popular vote.

It is understandable that altering the Electoral College may seem daunting. Yet, such a transformation could prove significantly beneficial in the future. A direct election could incentivize candidates to make excessive promises merely to secure votes. Additionally, we must consider the potential for deadlocks and ensuring equity within a direct election framework. Hence, while a complete departure from the Electoral College may not be ideal, addressing its deficiencies is imperative.

Presently, electors wield disproportionate influence over the presidential outcome. The "winner-takes-all" structure inherent within the Electoral College disadvantages voters. Candidates disproportionately focus on a limited number of "swing" states, neglecting others. Consider, for instance, the 2000 election cycle when seventeen states were bypassed. Due to such shortcomings, the Electoral College is antiquated and warrants either reform or complete overhaul.

Candidates strategically select electors to bolster their electoral prospects. These electors serve to garner support without necessitating the candidate's physical presence in every state. Essentially, the candidate awaits the final outcome. A candidate could conceivably prevail without expending substantial effort, as electors ultimately determine the victor. This arrangement creates opportunities for candidates to potentially sway electors in pivotal states through bribery.

When citizens cast their ballots, they are indirectly voting for electors rather than directly for the candidates. Electors are chosen via various means, such as through state conventions or by the candidates themselves. A salient flaw within the Electoral College is its susceptibility to unforeseen "disaster" scenarios. As exemplified in 1960, when individuals in Louisiana contemplated shifting Democratic electors to those opposed to John F. Kennedy.

In conclusion, the Electoral College is an outdated mechanism for conducting elections. It grants undue authority to electors, as opposed to the citizenry. It may have been justifiable in a less enlightened era, yet it is no longer conducive to our modern democracy.

Alright, listen up folks! We see cars all the time. Sure, they're easy, but what if we cut back on using them? Imagine: better air and more cash in your wallet. Honestly, driving less is a pretty bright idea.

Think about Paris, for example. Robert Duffer pointed out: "Paris bans driving due to smog." Their fix was pretty smart. One day, cars with odd numbers on their plates could drive; the next day, it was the even numbers. If you broke the rules, you paid a fine. They even offered free rides on buses and trains for a whole week! Traffic and smog? Gone! The air got cleaner, and people saved some money. Duffer mentioned that traffic jams dropped by 60%! He said, "Traffic jams dropped 60 percent in Paris, after five days of really bad smog... almost as bad as Beijing, China, which is famous for being super polluted." The smog was terrible, but fewer cars made a BIG difference.

Also, check out Vauban, Germany. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars." They went all in, almost getting rid of cars completely. Around 70% don't even have one, because parking costs a lot outside the town, and cars are mostly not allowed. They really like it. Heidrun Walter said "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Not many young people even bother to get their license! Rosenthal wrote in "The end of car culture" that cars are now just things you use to get around. Nobody cares much about the car itself, just getting where they need to go without hassle.

Basically, cars help you get places, but so do buses, trains, bikes, and sharing rides. Some of these are even free! And you get less pollution and traffic? That's a win-win for our health and for the planet.

In Plumer's view (Source 2), the Electoral College is undeniably unfair, outdated, and lacks logical justification. Many concur, viewing it as either a potential catastrophe or inherently flawed. While some defend it as a safeguard against tied elections or as a means of empowering larger states, these arguments are often met with skepticism. Advocates for abolishing the Electoral College strongly prefer a straightforward popular vote, perceiving it as a superior alternative.

A significant issue arises from the unequal weight of votes. Electors wield disproportionate influence compared to individual citizens. Direct voting would ensure that each vote carries equal weight. Furthermore, the possibility of electors being influenced or compromised undermines the very essence of democracy.

Many consider the Electoral College to be an artifact of a bygone era. Plumer (Source 2) references Bob Dole's call to "Abolish the Electoral College!" This sentiment is widely shared, with many favoring direct voting over decisions made by a select group. Some even quip that a child randomly throwing darts could produce a more sensible outcome. Plumer (Source 2) notes, "Over 60 percent of voters want a direct election," and this preference has intensified since 2000.

Frankly, the Electoral College's system is peculiar. It's a confusing process where electors, not the populace, directly choose the president. Plumer (Source 2) explains, "You vote for electors, who then vote for the president. Who are they? Usually, just regular folks. Who picks them? It depends on the state – maybe a party group, or the candidate. Can we control how they vote? Not always! Do people accidentally vote for the wrong electors? Sometimes!" This complicated procedure raises questions about who truly holds the power.

While the Electoral College might offer some advantages, such as promoting stability, the prevailing view is that its drawbacks outweigh its benefits. A simple popular vote is perceived as a much fairer approach. As Plumer (Source 2) aptly summarizes, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational!"

The Electoral College, a system from our country's beginnings, is written into the Constitution. I believe we ought to keep it because it has been a good system to this point, and there is no reason to change it. Retaining the Electoral College is a good idea, as it supports us in about five key instances.

There are admittedly some downsides to the Electoral College. For instance, Electors could disregard their party and vote unexpectedly. We actually vote for electors, who then go on to select the president. Those electors could just brush off the popular vote and cast their own votes as they please. Beyond that, a state might submit two different lists of electors, and that would cause a real mess. And while a tie is uncommon in the overall count, the Electoral College perpetually carries that risk. Due to the "winner-takes-all" approach, candidates often don't bother campaigning in some states because one party has a huge lead. With a direct vote, candidates might campaign harder in every state to gain additional votes. However, criticisms of the Electoral College often address unusual scenarios; therefore, looking at the alternate perspective is smart.

Swing states hold all the power in presidential elections; as such, their voters tend to be more knowledgeable. Knowing that their votes are important, they pay close attention to the candidates and the news. In order to sway these states, candidates spend considerable time and resources there. Since one party is so dominant, many other states are all but guaranteed before voting even commences. Voters in swing states potentially weigh who they like and what they believe in more carefully; this could mean that they are the best voters to elect our president.

The Electoral College prevents the need for extra elections; this decreases confusion. In its absence, a direct vote that is very close might trigger significant problems. For instance, the Electoral College's importance is displayed through Nixon's victory in 1968 and Clinton's in 1992, where they won with just approximately 43% of the votes.

I can understand why some voters in states that are heavily affiliated with one side might dislike the Electoral College. As their votes may feel irrelevant, they might not participate in campaigns. One person's vote does not have much individual weight, and in 2012, not everyone even voted. For many, simply voicing their preferred candidate is more important than impacting the entire election. Your vote is more likely to be impactful when you are actively involved in the workings of our government.

Dear Senator,

Listen, we have to keep the Electoral College around! It's got 538 members. To win the presidency, someone needs over 270 votes. States choose these electors, who are usually tied to a candidate. Keeping this system? It’s really important. It makes sure there's a clear winner and a president for everyone.

A big reason to keep it? It almost always gives one person a clear victory. Yeah, a tie *could* happen, but it's not likely, even with an even number of electors. In 2012, Obama got about 62% of the electoral votes, but only 51% of the popular vote. Most states? Winner takes *all* the electoral votes. Even a small lead can mean a big electoral win. But this "winner-take-all" thing means people mostly go after votes in swing states and ignore the others. Still, the winner usually gets way more electoral votes than actual votes.

Also, the President has to stand for *everyone*. The Electoral College makes candidates get votes from everywhere. No single place has enough votes to choose the president alone. Even if you're really popular in one area, you still have to campaign in other states. More votes in your easy states don't get you more electoral votes. A president without support everywhere? That's not good. People might think their votes don't matter or the president doesn't care about them.

The truth is: we *must* keep the Electoral College. It stops ties and makes sure the president cares about *every* American.

Heidrun Walter mentioned that owning a car caused her constant stress, but now she cherishes the newfound freedom of a car-free lifestyle. This reflects a rising trend: people are choosing to move away from car dependence. Though cars were once viewed as essential for mobility, declining car sales and reduced driving indicate that people are beginning to see the advantages of reducing car use. Notably, this shift can aid in mitigating the greenhouse effect, decrease smog and air pollution, and support diverse economic sectors beyond just cars and transport.

The greenhouse effect, worsened by car emissions, endangers both nature and human lives. According to the article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," cars contribute to 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions, and nearly 50% in certain US regions. Reducing car usage could lead to emissions cuts, benefitting the planet. This, in turn, could positively impact major issues such as global warming and rising sea levels. Therefore, less car use can have a positive effect as it can curtail greenhouse gas emissions.

Smog and air pollution pose significant threats, harming both our health and the environment. Robert Duffer’s article, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," stated that "After Days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city." This five-day ban succeeded in improving air quality. Increased similar actions could diminish pollution and smog levels. It might also encourage people to adopt alternative transportation methods such as buses, bikes, and walking. "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" reveals that suspending car use in a city can foster more bike paths, parks, sports centers, improved sidewalks, and refined traffic management. Hence, using cars less can decrease smog and air pollution, leading to improved urban living.

Finally, minimizing car use can stimulate the economy in various ways. One source indicated that a significant portion of US transport funding has been directed towards highways. Reducing car dependence could unlock funds for initiatives like schools, government programs, and local projects. Moreover, as another source suggests, reduced car use can conserve resources, enabling individuals to allocate funds to other areas. The declining driving rates are a testament to this shift. As noted by one source, "Demographic shifts in the driving population suggest that the trend may accelerate. There has been a large drop in the percentage of 16 to 39yearolds getting a license." This allows for a greater focus on economic improvement. Simply put, less car use yields economic gains, such as increased government spending capacity, personal savings, and economic growth.

In conclusion, the benefits of reducing car use are numerous. These limitations can stimulate economic growth, curb greenhouse gas emissions, and alleviate smog and air pollution in densely populated cities.

Selecting a president carries immense weight, as their actions possess the power to reshape the nation, for positive or negative outcomes. The electoral college mechanism endeavors to capture the sentiments of each state, albeit its efficacy is a subject of contention. In my view, it serves as a reasonable gauge of the prevailing preference within each state, thereby reflecting the overall mood of the country.

Criticism is leveled against the electoral college, asserting its failure to elect the most qualified president and questioning its fairness. Nonetheless, we retain the privilege of CHOICE! Our voices still hold considerable significance. Ultimately, each vote holds value, far surpassing the alternative of abstaining. While imperfections exist, complete satisfaction remains elusive. Thus, I contend that the electoral college adequately fulfills its function of aggregating votes.

Complexity surrounds the endeavor to devise a superior alternative. The primary concern revolves around the electoral college's perceived inability to accurately represent the collective will. However, I disagree. By casting a vote, individuals endeavor to secure the election of their preferred candidate. Such votes contribute to shaping the electoral college's direction. This fosters collaboration among states, as the majority vote reflects each state's preference, an aspect I find appealing.

Concerns are voiced regarding the disproportionate influence wielded by larger states within the electoral college. While this may hold some validity, it is perhaps justifiable. States with larger populations should rightfully command a greater number of votes. However, the equal weighting of each individual vote mitigates the potential for excessive dominance. Despite dissenting opinions, the electoral college provides an equitable means of gauging the overall sentiment of the nation, aligning with expectations.

In conclusion, the electoral college effectively captures the preferences of each state by employing the majority vote to determine the presidential victor. While it is not without flaws, a simple aggregation of individual votes could potentially yield less desirable outcomes. All systems possess inherent shortcomings, yet the electoral college represents a relatively organized method for tabulating votes over time. Universal approval may remain unattainable, as complete consensus is rarely achievable!

Dear Senator, So, in pondering over the optimal method to select our President and their administration, a thought has crystallized. We're essentially faced with two paths: the Electoral College, or a straightforward tally of all votes cast. Upon thorough reflection, it seems to me that one of these options shines far brighter than the other.

Under the Electoral College system, electors convene to cast their votes for the President and Vice President. These 538 individuals collectively determine the outcome of the election. Frankly, this arrangement doesn't sit well with me. We operate under the illusion of directly electing the president, when in reality, we're merely selecting electors who ultimately make the decision. Why should a select group, dwarfed by the millions of voters, wield such disproportionate power in choosing the nation's leader, leaving the rest of us in suspense? It strikes me as unjust that their opinions carry so much more weight than those of ordinary citizens when it comes to deciding who governs the country.

In contrast, a popular vote system would empower every citizen to directly vote for their preferred presidential candidate and team. This approach allows us to unequivocally express our choice for leadership, with the aspiration of positive change. Even in the event of a candidate's defeat, your vote retains its significance, contributing to the collective voice of the nation. While the Electoral College might offer a swifter resolution, the knowledge that your vote genuinely matters could galvanize greater citizen participation and encourage deeper engagement with the candidates and their platforms. We gain the agency to shape our future, rather than simply hoping an elector aligns with our views.

I acknowledge that the Electoral College has certain merits, such as ensuring a definitive victor. Some might argue that selecting electors is functionally equivalent to direct voting. However, these minor advantages pale in comparison to the imperative of allowing every citizen to directly exercise their democratic right to vote.

In essence, when deliberating on the presidential selection process, we must confront a fundamental question: How do you envision your voice being heard? Do you aspire to direct involvement, or are you content to delegate your decision to others? For me, the answer is unequivocal: I advocate for the system that amplifies the chorus of our democracy.

Think about a pleasant family walk in the park. Unfortunately, pollution is making this difficult. We rely too heavily on cars instead of choosing buses, walking, or carpooling. Reducing our driving is incredibly important. Owning a car is costly, discourages other transportation methods, and harms the environment.

Unexpected expenses can arise from owning a car. Parking spots in Vauban, Germany, can cost an astounding $40,000 – exceeding the price of some homes! In Bogota, Columbia, driving on restricted days incurs a $25 fine. Driving in Paris may result in a 22-euro fine based on the license plate number. Moreover, gas prices are consistently high, which may not bother the wealthy, but it is a big concern for families with children.

Often, the advantages of public transportation are overlooked. Imagine a world without buses, trains, or subways; simply try driving into New York City! Bogota encourages bus or walking by fining drivers on car-free days. Mayor Antanas Mockus celebrated high participation, even in the rain. Other cities, like Cali and Valledupar, followed suit, attracting international interest. New York even has a bike-sharing program. Public transportation is crucial for environmental preservation, despite some skepticism.

Smog sometimes obscures the stars. Paris temporarily banned cars due to excessive pollution, resulting in a 60% improvement in five days! However, France's preference for diesel fuel, a major pollutant, complicates matters. Bogota's car-free day offers a respite from smog, while Vauban's limited car usage minimizes it. Small changes can significantly benefit the environment.

Vauban and Bogota demonstrate that reduced driving alleviates pollution. Paris resorts to car bans to combat smog. Vauban discourages car use through expensive parking, and Bogota imposes fines, encouraging walking or bus travel. Reducing our dependence on cars is essential because it saves money, promotes alternative transportation, and purifies the air.

Our town stands to gain if we reduce car dependence. Cities globally, such as those in France and Colombia, are encouraging alternative transportation. Individuals report reduced stress when provided with transportation options beyond driving. Domestic studies also indicate a decline in car ownership. Now is the ideal moment to embrace innovative strategies from places like Vauban or Bogota, and diminish time wasted in traffic jams.

Consider this: a German neighborhood demonstrates the feasibility of car-free living. Elisabeth Rosenthal highlighted Vauban, Germany, as largely "car-free." Public transport is favored by most residents. The article further noted, "70% of families don't have cars, and many sell theirs upon relocating there!" One resident expressed feeling more relaxed and content without a car, due to the absence of driving-related stress. Numerous locations are aspiring to emulate this model, decreasing car usage to benefit the environment. Expect more "smart planning" initiatives to emerge. Through collective effort to reduce driving, our town can become a car-free paradise.

Bogota, Colombia, promotes car-free days to encourage alternative travel methods and enhance air quality. First-time observers are often struck by the city's improved atmosphere. Embracing a "car-free" lifestyle promotes increased physical activity and city beautification. Reports highlight the proliferation of parks and sports facilities, spacious and pristine sidewalks, along with vibrant shops and eateries. The benefits extend beyond cleaner air, transforming the city into a more enjoyable and visually appealing environment!

Imagine implementing strategies similar to those in Bogota and Vauban. Car purchases are already dwindling locally. Some individuals are even forgoing driver's education. One author noted a decline in American car enthusiasm. Another indicated that peak mileage was reached some time ago, with subsequent reductions. Young people are pivotal to this shift. Their increased reliance on social media, phones, carpools, and public transit results in decreased driving. Although car manufacturers may face challenges, many anticipate improved and more accessible travel options as a result.

Ultimately, diminished driving translates to reduced pollution and stress, improved neighborhoods, increased exercise, and resource conservation. Globally, cities are striving to curb car usage to combat pollution. By embracing a green and car-free approach, we will become part of a global transformation!

Dear Senator, I am reaching out to urge the abolition of the Electoral College. In my strong opinion, the presidency should be determined solely by the national popular vote count. Candidly, I view the Electoral College as fundamentally unjust and a significant danger to the core principles of the U.S. Constitution.

The Electoral College undermines the very essence of our nation's democracy. While we do cast our ballots, the Electoral College possesses the power to override our collective decision, a notion that feels profoundly disrespectful! It raises serious doubts about the true nature of our democracy. Reflect on the 2000 election: Gore secured the popular vote, yet Bush ultimately prevailed due to the Electoral College. As "The Indefensible Electoral College" aptly points out, "Over 60% of voters want a direct election, not this system." The sentiment is clear: the majority of Americans desire a straightforward voting process.

Furthermore, my profound dislike for the Electoral College stems from the risk it poses to the U.S. Constitution, especially our cherished rights. The foundational principle is that we, the citizenry, elect our president. However, when the candidate with the most votes is defeated by the Electoral College, it is a blow to democracy. "The Indefensible Electoral College" expresses concern about irregular elections, posing the scenario: "What if a state sends in two sets of electors? It happened in Hawaii in '60. Luckily, Nixon okayed only the electors for his rival." Envision the potential turmoil if this scenario were to reoccur and go unchecked, handing a candidate an undeserved victory! The Electoral College represents a looming catastrophe.

Thus, let us abolish the Electoral College! It is inherently unfair and poses a grave threat to our U.S. Constitution. I implore you to carefully consider this perspective as you deliberate on this pivotal matter.

The amount of air pollution produced as people travel is a major issue worldwide. The amount of environmental improvement that could be achieved if people used cars less frequently is likely underestimated by many. Cars are constantly emitting harmful pollutants. Therefore, it is crucial to carefully consider the implications before deciding to buy a car. The perceived benefits of car ownership are dwarfed by the detrimental environmental effects.

Consider the impact of cars, buses and big trucks. They are notorious consumers of gasoline, which is detrimental to the environment. The fuels we burn are endangering the planet! Continuing on our current path, ozone depletion could result from this pollution. Ultimately, we might be left with no place to live. Furthermore, people often over-rely on cars. Instead of opting to walk or bike, they frequently drive, exacerbating the issue. Consider the driving restrictions implemented in Paris to mitigate pollution. As reported by sources, there was an impressive drop in traffic of 60% after only five days of intense smog! To say the least, the environmental impact of transportation could be described as a "death wish."

Owning a car comes with a myriad of hidden costs in addition to the initial purchase. In Vauban, Germany, the requirement of purchasing a parking spot in order to own a car, is quite interesting as mentioned by some sources. A parking spot can cost $40,000 on top of the cost of the house! Don't forget about the high price of gasoline. Car insurance just adds to the financial burden. In addition, driving is often dangerous, stressful, and unpleasant.

Cars? We're surrounded! They define modern life. Ditching them? That'd be a HUGE win for our planet! Rampant car use? Think thicker pollution and nasty, unbreathable air.

Bogota, Colombia had a "No Car Day"— a pretty neat concept ripe for broader adoption. Colombians traded four wheels for feet, bikes, and buses, massively decongesting the city! A little rain? No problem! Most people participated anyway. The mayor was quoted as stating the weather was a non-issue. The goal? Cleaner air, powered by alternative transport. Scaling this to the U.S., however, might be tricky, considering our deep-seated car love.

Paris tackled car-induced air pollution by banning driving during peak smog. Initial reaction? Less than thrilled. Reuters reported almost 4,000 citations and 27 vehicle impoundments! The upside? Traffic plummeted a whopping 60%! Despite this, Beijing-esque smog persisted. The long-term acceptance of these regulations is up in the air.

Restricting cars could trigger backlash: mobility compromised! BUT, fresh studies suggest fewer Americans are buying cars, driving, or even getting licenses. A Drexel University professor believes this might signal a fundamental shift. Circumstances forced driving cessation; maybe they won't revert. US car usage could remain curtailed, thus improving air quality.

Unfettered car use wreaks environmental havoc, globally. Reducing reliance is tough, fraught with challenges. However, the potential benefits are undeniable. Envision cleaner air, more pedestrians, cyclists, and skaters. Taming car culture? That unlocks a better life, environmentally speaking. Driving less? A planetary lifeline!

If reducing your car use has any impact depends on your location and just how much you dedicate yourself to it. All around the globe, experiments are underway that investigate towns with fewer or even no cars. Areas that have incredibly high levels of pollution often reduce the amount of car usage a lot to try and improve air quality.

Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb..." really makes clear what life is like without a vehicle. The infrastructure being in place makes walking and biking more convenient. This shift holds significant weight. Due to the pollution issue, trying this out in other parts of the globe appears to be a good idea.

Some cities face seriously terrible pollution issues. Duffer's writing, "Paris bans driving...", brings up the smog in Paris, and how it brought about a ban on driving. Because the people were outraged, those who broke the rules were given fines and had their vehicles impounded. Duffer stresses that "Congestion was down 60 percent...", which demonstrates how helpful the ban was in decreasing traffic.

All over the world, different programs are trying to push people into using modes of transportation that don't create pollution. How well it went in Bogota is shown in Selsky's "Carfree day..." Millions of people took to walking, biking, or skating even when it rained. As Selsky mentions, Bogota also feels more inviting. "Parks…sidewalks” have been upgraded, which improves the city as a whole, and benefits everyone.

We have to adjust to the fast-paced changes taking place around the world. Young people are beginning to question whether owning a vehicle is actually a necessity, opting for choices that are cheaper and more environmentally conscious. The belief that everyone needs a car is fading. While still around, it is becoming less common.

The Electoral College? Still debated! Should we keep it or get rid of it? It's kinda weird, right? Why have a system where we vote for someone who *then* votes for the president? If America wants citizens to pick the president, why doesn't the popular vote just win?

When you vote for a president, what are you *really* doing? Are you picking the people who will vote? Seriously? The article "Does the Electoral College Work?" says we're voting for "your candidate's electors." The Constitution says we can vote for our president, but how does this make sense? It feels wrong for America and its voting system. And can we even trust these electors? "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "...Can voters control whom their electors vote for? Not always." So, the person you want might *not* get your vote? The Electoral College could stop you from voting directly and weaken your right to have your vote matter! It's confusing, and your voice should be heard!

Some say the Electoral College makes sure we get a president, but does it pick the one most people want? Usually not. The popular vote often feels useless, like our voices don't matter. It feels like losing an American right. The popular vote should win, not the Electoral College. Al Gore lost even though he won the popular vote. "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "...according to a Gallup poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gorethanks to the quirks of the electoral collegewon the popular vote but lost the presidency..." This shows how it makes our votes less important. The same article says "...over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now." If votes *really* matter, we should vote on whether to keep it.

Some people think the Electoral College keeps things in order and stops voting problems. But is that true? Every system has problems, but the Electoral College isn't the answer. America was built on the idea that we listen to the people's vote, and I want to see that happen. I want to vote directly for who I want, not vote for someone else to vote.

Basically, the Electoral College is unfair, broken, and un-American. It stops us from voting for who we want and makes us vote for electors. If you believe in American values, you can see how wrong this is. It's a bad way to handle votes, and the American people should be in charge!

Esteemed Senator,

Why the Electoral College still, you ask? Some say it wrecks real democracy, saying we pick electors, not the leader we want. But really, it works! It helps find the winner fast and gives us a president for all.

It's here to make sure we get a clear win. Just voting for who's most liked could make things worse! Richard A. Posner says the winner almost always gets way more Electoral College votes than just regular votes. This makes fights over who won less likely. He's right; those Electoral College votes make things easy. Plus, it stops ties, like a Nixon-Clinton tie in '92. Because it counts more votes, the Electoral College helps make the winner clear.

Also, it makes the president think about everyone. Candidates need votes everywhere, not just where people like them. We don't want a president who only cares about one area! Imagine Republicans only liking the South. If a Republican just cares about the South, that's not fair to the rest of us. Bradford Plumer says candidates forget about states they probably won't win. But maybe those states are already decided! Why would a Democrat try in Texas, where Republicans win? Or a Republican in California, where Democrats win? The voters probably already chose! Old ideas like Plumer's might be wrong now. Things change! If you don't like the Electoral College, you need new info. Look at the last few elections! What people thought in 2004, 2008, and 2012 depended on what was happening, what they were promised, and if young people voted. So, the Electoral College makes sure the president cares about the whole country.

So, keep the Electoral College! It says who won and helps the president speak for all of us. Electoral College votes are usually higher, so it's easier to see who won. Plus, it makes the president think about the whole country, not just some places. Senator, please keep it! It's worked well lately, so it'll probably keep working.

A long time ago, people walked to get around. Stores, food, and family visits all involved walking. But now, why do we love cars so much? Cities everywhere are trying to get us to stop driving.

Cars hurt the environment from the start. But we're smarter now! New rules are appearing to limit car use. In Vauban, Germany, people live without cars to help the Earth, and they like it! Heidrun Walter, a mom there, said, "Cars made me stressed. Now I'm much happier!"

Paris, the "City of Love," got rid of cars because of bad air. Traffic jams dropped a lot—60 percent—in just five days!

Paris had the worst air in Europe, full of smog, so they had to do something. The ban worked well, cleaning the air and getting rid of smog.

Big cities like New York, with its small streets, get very crowded with cars. Buildings are close together, so walking or biking is faster than driving. New York's bike program proves this. Also, carpooling is becoming popular, where people share rides to reduce the number of cars. This means less pollution, fewer traffic jams, and more money saved on gas.

Bill Ford, the head of Ford, wants a future where owning a car is annoying. He wants to work with phone companies to create cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all work together, saving time, energy, reducing pollution, and making things safer." We can't just ban cars everywhere because they are often more useful than trains or walking, but using them less is better. Too many cars cause a lot of pollution! What if we had one car-free day everywhere and tried other ways to travel? Biking is great exercise and faster than walking, so you can get in shape and arrive earlier!

Presidential races? The Electoral College turns them into a crazy battle. Is it on its way out, or here to stay? It's like mixing votes from the people with votes from Congress. Elections in the past had some mess-ups, and future ones could be even more insane. Why can't we just vote directly for the president in a country that is supposed to be a democracy? We need to find something better, and, frankly, ditch the Electoral College.

Here's the big deal: we choose electors, and guess what? They decide who becomes president, not us. So, even if the popular vote wins, there are still problems, like the "winner-take-all" thing. "The Indefensible Electoral College" says this setup is no good for voters. Candidates just skip states they can't win, focusing on the states that could go either way. They set up shop in some states and totally ignore others. Like, in 2000, seventeen states didn't even get a visit, proving just how flawed the Electoral College is.

And here's another scary thought: "the disaster factor." Bradford Plumer said we got lucky in 2000, but the system could have way bigger problems. In Louisiana, some people almost replaced the Democratic electors with folks against John F. Kennedy to screw with the election. Can you believe it? That shows how messed up the system could be if they had gotten away with it. Or, we could see states sending different electors or a tie in the vote. Even with ways to fix these disasters, it still makes you want a much better system.

Richard A. Posner is a defender of the Electoral College and thinks it keeps things fair. He says it gives smaller states more power to make things even with the bigger states. But that isn't really balancing; it's giving an advantage to smaller states. Posner also thinks voters in swing states get more involved. Plumer doesn't think so. He points out that campaigns ignored places like Rhode Island and South Carolina. A lot of voters in big TV markets didn't even see any campaign ads. The Electoral College can be easily manipulated, and we should scrap it.

Look, I still think the Electoral College has to go. People should be able to pick their president. Even if it is difficult, the current system is very obviously crooked. Some people say the Electoral College is fine, but that is really not the case. We need a complete overhaul or some seriously heavy-duty changes to the system.

Honorable Florida State Senator, The Electoral College? It's a hot debate! Some champion it as a sturdy method for choosing presidents, while others condemn it as fundamentally unfair and obsolete. A superior solution is needed to address these concerns. Shouldn't a true democracy have a voting system that authentically embodies its principles?

Defenders of the Electoral College highlight infrequent tie outcomes, or the supposed faithfulness of electors to the popular vote. But hey, rarity isn't an excuse for ignoring issues! Minor system flaws can snowball, you know, like a tiny snag in your favorite shirt. Neglecting Electoral College fixes now risks overwhelming problems later. Electors *might* consider public opinion, but rogue choices are possible!

The Electoral College? It dilutes the impact of each of us. You cast your ballot, sure, but electors ultimately decide the winner. The uneven distribution of electors across states also feels… well, unfair. Like some votes are more valuable. This discourages participation; people feel unheard! Plus, candidates ignore states they can't win, focusing only on electoral vote havens. Disconnected voters? Check.

Beyond citizen disempowerment, the Electoral College screams issues. Electoral ties, though rare, can happen. The House of Representatives then steps in. Now, that could create a choice that clashes with what the people actually wanted. Oh, and you can even win the popular vote but still lose the whole election because of the Electoral College – remember Al Gore in 2000? See? It's a broken system. Fix it *before* more elections happen and make things worse.

The Electoral College functions, sure, but it doesn't mirror what the population desires. Electors, *not* you and me, pick the president. This is a problem for a government that is for the people. A better system is out there, one that truly jives with democracy’s values. Speak out! Ditch the overrated Electoral College and champion a system that perfectly embodies the American spirit.

While cars provide convenient transportation, their emissions pose a significant environmental threat. Reducing car usage emerges as an effective solution, yielding multiple benefits—reduced greenhouse gas emissions, decreased smog levels, and conservation of vital resources.

Primarily, diminishing reliance on automobiles substantially curtails greenhouse gas emissions. Consider cycling, opting for trains, or even pedestrian commutes. Car exhaust contributes significantly to greenhouse gas production, posing an environmental burden. According to Source 1, standard vehicles account for roughly half of these emissions. Consequently, reducing the number of cars on the road directly contributes to improving the planet's ecological well-being.

Furthermore, curtailing car usage noticeably diminishes smog. Smog jeopardizes both the natural world and human health. Envision smog as noxious air that causes illness. Many cars contribute to a severe rise in smog in certain areas. Source 2 highlighted that Paris almost encountered more severe air pollution levels than China, a country with notorious air quality issues. Air quality improves drastically as we minimize driving. The proof? A considerable 60% drop in smog levels occurred in Paris with just five days of reduced car activity. Thus, the result of cleaner air is a major payoff of decreased automobile use.

Lastly, less car use aids in the conservation of crucial resources. Source 1 suggests that diminished car use may result in smaller urban developments. This translates to less land consumption for roads, commercial centers, and residential areas. Source 4 asserts that this scenario would conserve vast amounts of construction materials and protect our natural resources. Overall, it's clear that a reduction in driving creates an improved life for all.

Essentially, a decreased dependence on cars provides ecological benefits, advances human health, and protects precious resources for generations.

While there's a significant push for its abolition, the Electoral College offers, surprisingly, more advantages than disadvantages. The Constitution purposefully established this mechanism, aiming to balance power between Congress and the populace in presidential elections. This system guarantees broad representation across the nation, empowering state leaders to champion their constituents' interests. Despite criticism, it stands as a viable method for electing the President.

The Electoral College compels presidential hopefuls to cultivate nationwide appeal. A regional base, such as the South, lacks sufficient electoral votes to secure victory. Consequently, candidates must endeavor to attract support from less favorable regions. This is beneficial, as a President with broad appeal is more effective. Intense focus on swing states galvanizes and educates voters, making them well-equipped to determine the election's outcome.

Preserving the Electoral College is wise because it typically aligns with the popular vote. The 2000 election saw Al Gore win the popular vote, yet George W. Bush prevailed due to the Electoral College. Such instances are infrequent, with the previous occurrence in 1888. Electors, selected by parties, generally vote for their party's nominee, which is what they are expected to do. Abandoning the Electoral College in favor of a popular vote system might cause new issues. Further considerations on a tie? We have even numbered votes, yikes! Although extremely rare, a tie would not be good, though it has never happened. The Electoral College also resolves scenarios where no candidate secures a majority.

Critics contend that the Electoral College discourages voting in states perceived as unwinnable for a given candidate, like Republicans in California. Nevertheless, every vote influences the allocation of a state's electoral votes. People participate in presidential elections to express their preferences, not solely to cast the deciding vote. The Electoral College has functioned effectively for over a century, and its continuation promises sustained success.

Saving the planet is simple: reduce driving. Get this: a European study shows cars cause ~12% of greenhouse gases. Ditching your car may seem hard. But wait, there are many choices! Carpool, get a ride, take a bus/train, use a taxi, or bike! It's not about choices; it's about wanting to help the planet.

Cars harm the Earth! Cities are fighting back using fees to cut car use. Paris tried this. After smog, they banned cars for a while! This shows the world can improve! Beijing, once as polluted as Paris, was cleaned up by the government. Bogota bans 7.2+ million from driving on "Day Without Cars." Violators face a $25 fine or jail. Andrew Selsky noted this "program set to spread to other countries, millions of Colombians hiked, biked, skated, or took buses to work during this car free day". It's not just cleaner air, but people socializing in parks.

Fewer cars truly benefit you! Less traffic means cleaner air. More walking reduces heart problems, obesity, etc.! Car companies might dislike this. Ford, Toyota, Honda, et al., could lose money if car sales drop, but many will still drive. However, less car production might boost the economy by cutting gas costs. Guess what? Driving is decreasing yearly! Experts see this as great for Earth, despite car company concerns.

Look: Car use is declining! Young adults don't buy cars. A 2013 study saw a 23% drop in young people's driving from 2001-2009. Experts are calm, but the sales drop raises questions about whether the U.S. has hit "peak driving." What's next? Good or bad? Unclear! Obama’s goals to lower greenhouse gases seem effective. Studies now suggest that "Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by".

Senator, the Electoral College is wild, huh? Should we keep it or switch to a system where whoever gets the most votes wins? I'm leaning toward the popular vote. Lots of people don't even bother voting now, maybe half, because they think their vote doesn't matter in states like California or Texas where the winner seems pre-picked, according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. Also, candidates only care about "swing states," forgetting everyone else.

Also, Senator, "only half of eligible Americans voted in 2012," per "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. People don't see the point if their candidate has no chance in their state. Some even tried to mess with the Electoral College. Segregationists tried to screw over Democratic Electors to hurt Kennedy, read "The Indefensible Electoral College" Section 11, showing how shaky it is.

I know there are reasons to keep the Electoral College. Some say it's a compromise by the Founding Fathers to be fair, giving "qualified citizens in congress" a say, according to "What is the Electoral College" Section 1. Maybe it helps because people influence electors, and history matters, says "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 15. Ideally, electors vote for the winner, per "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16. But even if electoral votes are right, the popular vote can still be higher—"In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16.

Senator, the winner-take-all thing is unfair. Candidates spend all their time and money in big states, running ads, and ignore smaller states they think they've won. "Swing states" get all the attention. "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad," says "What is wrong with the Electoral College" Section 13. They care about big and "swing states," which bums out voters in ignored states.

So, Senator, let's switch to a popular vote. It would make people want to vote, knowing their vote matters and helps pick who runs things. "Power to the People." That's what's important.

Man, cars are everywhere now! It feels like nobody walks or bikes; everyone just drives. This changes a lot of things. Is this how it should be? Rosenthal, Duffer, and Selsky understand this issue. They all want fewer cars, but for different reasons. After reading their work, it's obvious: fewer cars lead to more free time, better businesses, saved resources, and a weaker "car culture."

Are you always late because of traffic? Maybe walking or biking would be faster! Selsky talked about a car-free day in Colombia. Millions "hiked, biked, skated or took buses," so the roads were "eerily... devoid of traffic jams." Less traffic means less waiting around! Walking is good for the Earth and saves time, too. Businesses can do better with fewer cars. Selsky says "new restaurants and upscale shopping districts" appear, and old sidewalks become "broad, smooth sidewalks." When people walk more, they see more stores and buy more things!

Saving resources is another big reason to use cars less. Rosenthal says cars cause "12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in some car-heavy areas." This is a problem! We *have* to drive less because car fumes are bad for the planet. Some cities even try "no-driving" days. Duffer said Paris had a "partial driving ban" after "days of near-record pollution." Less driving means less pollution, traffic, and smog! That's great.

Car culture might be fading away. Studies show that young Americans are driving less often. Rosenthal mentions a "23 percent" drop in driving among young people. Maybe it's tickets, parents, or concern for the planet. But Rosenthal also said experts believe this will help reduce "carbon emissions and the environment." Because of this, car companies are changing, and tech is changing commute times.

In short: less driving means more time, better businesses, saved resources, and a weaker car culture. Next time you travel, consider driving less. It's much better for the Earth.

Alright, check it: the Electoral College? You gotta keep it, period. People complain it's outdated, but it's still the top way to pick a Prez, no debate. Picture this: elections are way smoother than if we just tallied every vote. No recounts needed. And the big thing? It keeps big states from bossing everyone around. So, keep the Electoral College? It stops total madness.

Electoral College wins almost never cause the wild fights you'd get if we just counted every single vote. Often, candidates get way more electoral votes than what their popular vote would tell you. Think Obama. He grabbed almost 62% of the electoral votes, even though only like 51% actually voted for him. With the "winner-take-all" rule in most states, figuring out each state's electoral votes is easy when someone gets the most votes, even if it’s not the majority. An Electoral College tie? It could happen, sure, but it's super rare, even with an even number of total electoral votes. Tallying popular votes? That would cause way more chaos.

The Electoral College stops us from needing runoff elections. Those happen when no one gets over 50%. Nixon in '68 and Clinton in '92 both got only about 43 percent of the popular vote, but they still won a majority in the Electoral College with 301 and 370 votes respectively. The Electoral College picks a clear winner, saving us the stress of a second vote. Because of it, runoff votes are totally not a thing.

So, bottom line: the Electoral College really stops big states from being too powerful. For real, I think we gotta keep using the Electoral College, like always. Maybe there are better ideas floating around, but the Electoral College is the best system we've got working now.

Click! Mom snapped a photo with her iPhone as my grin spread across my face. This small plastic device, feels like total independence. Oh, to be young again! I was lying on my stomach in the kitchen, a toy car nearby as Mom cooked, the smell of garlic filling up the space. I finally got my driver's license! It's a big deal for teens, yeah, but there are cons. If we drove less, the air would be less polluted, and we'd all breathe better.

Now that I can drive, I hate waking up to see thick smog, which makes my eyes squint when I drive to school. My car makes it worse! Car fumes are a big factor for smog because they send greenhouse gasses into the air. Cars in the U.S. cause about half of these emissions (Source 1). Making gas? Also causes pollution! It's a waste of energy, and it puts out carbon. So, fuel production lets out greenhouse gasses, and then our cars let out MORE! Because of AP Environmental Science, I know too many greenhouse gasses are not good. They hold sunlight, and UV rays and then, bam! Global warming. Smog also looks gross. Paris was full of smog, and they banned cars to clear it. After one day without cars, the smog went away! People could breathe again (Source 2). Smog hurts Paris' tourism. Who wants to see Versailles when it's covered in pollution?

Using cars less is good for our bodies and minds, no question. Look at Vauban, Germany. They don't allow any cars. More than half of the people who moved there sold their cars, and they're happier! Heidrun Walker, a media trainer with two kids, said, "When I had a car, I felt stressed. I'm way happier now." (Source 1). Walker's story shows that not driving is good for your mind. And your body feels good! People walk, run, bike, or skate to get around. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars" each year, so people walk in the city (Source 3). Walking is great for your heart! If more places had days without cars, maybe there would be fewer people with heart issues.

So, driving less is good because it gives you cleaner air, healthier bodies, and happier minds. When you go to grab your keys to drive to your friend's, stop and think. Think about your health, think about the planet, and maybe walk instead.

Elections? Extremely important. They're not an everyday occurrence! Your voice becomes amplified with each vote, effectively influencing our nation's future course. Ideally, picking the president is as simple as a clear vote count; a straightforward path to identifying our next leader. Voting without constraints for who we genuinely support is essential. The simplicity of the popular vote easily trumps the Electoral College's inherent confusion.

Many argue that the popular vote carries a greater sense of fairness, stemming from its uncomplicated nature: simply vote, tally, and declare a winner. Candidates often try to visit and actively campaign in various states, if their schedules allow. Yet, as Bradford Plumer mentions, the Electoral College results in "candidates ignoring states they deem unwinnable, focusing on tight races in "swing" states." This seems unjust, since every state should have the opportunity to hear from candidates and understand their platforms. Some candidates neglect entire states. Plumer points out that "during the 2000 election, seventeen states were ignored by candidates, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the biggest media markets never saw a single campaign ad." How can voters make informed decisions without seeing or hearing candidates?

Furthermore, the popular vote is undeniably less complicated than the Electoral College, which can potentially lead to problematic situations. The popular vote offers simplicity: vote, count, and the person with the most votes wins. The Electoral College? Well, Plumer states that in the event of a tie, "the election is decided in the House of Representatives, with states voting for the president. The Senate then chooses the vice president. Because each state receives a single vote, Wyoming's sole vote, representing 500,000 people, carries equal weight to California's 55 representatives, who represent 35 million voters." Imagine the resulting chaos, a single vote holding immense power! The popular vote ensures clarity: everyone votes, and the most votes win. We don't need representatives to meddle and possibly derail the entire process.

Ultimately, the popular vote has the advantage because of its inherent fairness. We elect our preferred candidate without unnecessary interference. Plus, its much easier to understand than the Electoral College. A tie could result in representatives voting, even without understanding the needs of the population. As voting is such an important process, let's make sure we do it properly. We all should vote for our candidate of choice, preventing others from making the decision, including representatives. Voting is extremely important.

Okay, so, the Electoral College thing? The Federal Register folks say it's like this: We vote for electors. Then they meet and vote for the President and the VP. Congress counts up their votes. Basically, we pick people to pick the big guy. Sounds fine, yeah? What's the fuss?

Tons of people want to ditch it for just a straight popular vote. Bradford Plumer asks if those electors will even vote like we want them to. He thinks they probably won't! Okay, maybe sometimes, but we still pick electors we HOPE will pick right. Gotta trust 'em. Even if we voted straight for the President, we'd still have to trust THEY'D be good. It's almost the same, just one more step.

Richard A. Posner's got a point: "Why ditch a system we hate?" He figures the Electoral College makes things fairer, like how the Senate helps small states. Right on! Big states get little Senate say. So, if it was just popular vote, those big states would get even MORE attention 'cause they have all the people.

Also, the Electoral College stops things from getting wild if nobody gets over half the votes. Think 'bout '68. Nixon got the most votes (43%), but not a majority. Clinton was similar in '92. But they both won the Electoral College, so no runoff was needed. Phew!

I say keep the Electoral College. It works and stops Texas from bossing around Rhode Island. It helps us skip runoffs. Sure, some say it's old, but I think the Electoral College is a solid voting method.

Okay, here's the rewrite:

The electoral college? What a joke – old, unfair, and just plain dumb! Some try to defend it, but honestly, those defenses suck. Scared of direct voting? Seriously? Even Dole knew: ditch the electoral college! The best reasons *for* it are just wrong. And many forget – electors can't be trusted! You *think* they'll vote as promised, but guess what? No guarantee! Nixon, Carter, Dole, even the U.S. Chamber and the AFL-CIO – all wanted it gone at some point. People want the most votes to win, right? We vote for *electors*, not the president. And a tie? The House decides, but their votes AREN'T equal!

Electors? Not the same as *actually* voting for the president. Trust them to keep their word? Don't count on it! Voters pick the *wrong* electors? Yup! The electoral college? Broken, plain and simple. People think they're voting for the president, but SURPRISE! Lie. We don't even pick the electors *themselves*! Why think they're loyal? In 1960, Louisiana almost swapped electors to screw Kennedy. Crazy! This system? So easy to rig. The electoral college? A weak foundation. "Faithless" electors have betrayed their party. Could happen again. It's crooked! Ditch it! Let the people vote!

If the electoral vote is tied, the House picks the president and VP. Their system? Just as awful! So unfair. So undemocratic. Each state gets ONE vote. Meaning Wyoming's single rep, with, like, half a million people, equals California's 55 reps with 35 million! Makes sense? NO! That's the deal. People split their tickets, voting one party for president, another for Congress. So the House's choice could be totally wrong. Think ties are rare? Think again: 41,971 votes in '68 could've done it. 5,559 votes in Ohio and 3,687 in Hawaii in '76 would have been it. Small shifts, HUGE problems. "Your vote matters?" Seriously? With *this* system? Insane it's still here. People just want a simple vote.

Seriously: ditch the electoral college. Direct voting. That's the answer. Electors are flaky. The House is unequal. Let people vote fair! Let us vote! A president "For the people" should be picked "By the people."

Hey, driving all the time is a major bummer for our planet. Seriously, can we all just drive less? Earth is taking a beating, and it's messing with cities and everyone in them. Cars are a pain everywhere you look, from our town to Central America, even Europe. It is not just one place that deals with the problem. Burning gas is cooking the Earth, so how about we just try driving a little less?

So, paragraph 10 from the "Paris bans driving due to smog" article goes, "After days of near-record pollution..." So what if Paris is getting hit with some terrible pollution? It's getting horrible! The "City of Love" is choked in smog! If Paris is sending out smog alerts, what's happening in other cities? We have more of everything - factories, cars - all the things. What can we do about this? It's going to get worse if we don't fix it, so let's wake up and do something fast!

David Goldberg makes an awesome point in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph seven: "All of our development since World War II has been centered on the car..." Why are cars suddenly more important than us? I get that cars are fast, but what do we miss out on because of cars? Forget about gas; what about our health? Our money goes to roads and less to parks, and we see malls instead of nature. Trees are great for clean air, but pollution is taking them out. No one ever talked about smog before, but now cars are all anyone talks about!

Okay, so maybe we need to drive less, but it doesn't mean we are sunk! Maybe we could start with some no-car days. If you drive, you get a ticket! They do that in Bogota, Colombia! There's an article called "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," and maybe it'll work here! Cali and Valledupar are trying it out!

So, last summer, I finally took my first trip to California on the West Coast. I had this idea that it would be really diverse, like with surfing, mountains, and all that. When I got to Los Angeles, I expected a lively city, like Miami, but wow, was I wrong! Honestly, the smog and crazy traffic just ruined the natural beauty. That's normal in cities that rely on cars so much, but cutting back would really make a difference. Seriously, cleaner air and safer streets? Everyone would win!

Elizabeth Rosenthal mentions in source 1 that "Passenger cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions... and up to 50% in car-heavy US areas" (Rosenthal, paragraph 5). But it's not just cars; delivery trucks are making it worse. Now that everyone knows how bad greenhouse gases are, people really want to get rid of cars. Places like France, Colombia, and even the US are trying. And guess what? Source 4 says it's working! Rosenthal also notes that "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by" (paragraph 29). Sure, cars won't vanish, but cutting back can really help the planet.

Think about it: if we limited cars in crowded places like New York City, it could really reduce accidents. Did you know that in the US, there are about 500 car accidents every day? And worldwide, it's over 5000! Car accidents kill a lot of people, but many could be stopped. And let's be honest, overconfident drivers and people on their phones make it worse. But here's something cool: greenhouse gas reduction programs also help lower traffic accidents. Duffer says, "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France" (Duffer, paragraph 14). Also, Source 3 says that Bogota's Car Free Day is encouraging other Latin American countries to cut traffic and make it safer for people walking around.

Basically, people are starting to realize that using fewer cars is super important. Less car use means cleaner air and fewer accidents, simple as that. After I left the city and the highway on my California trip, I finally got to see all the beautiful nature!

So, what makes the air dirty anyway? Mainly cars. But, wait, driving less is actually super great! Seriously, picture this: cleaner air to breathe, more money to spend, and way safer streets for everyone. Let's dive in, shall we?

Okay, so the more cars, the dirtier the air gets, plain and simple. Remember Paris? When the air got nasty, they said "no more cars!" Boom, cleaner air! Less driving also means less burning by those big oil companies. Instead, you could hop on a bike, walk, grab a bus, skateboard, or even take the subway. Way cleaner!

And get this, your wallet will thank you! For real, those "25 fines" people got for driving where they shouldn't? Ouch! Owning a car? Gas is outrageous, plus tickets and fixing stuff. Less driving equals more cash for you over the long run.

Finally, let's talk about safety. One study showed mixing up how we travel – walking, biking, driving, buses – can make things safer. Less traffic means fewer accidents, plain and simple. Plus, less cars make it safer for bikes, motorcycles, and pedestrians. Oh, and it lowers the chances of drunk driving too!

Basically, ditching the car is awesome all around: cleaner air, more money in your pocket, and safer roads. So tell me, what makes *you* decide to leave the car at home once in a while?

It seems many people believe vehicles are essential these days. Even with their widespread use for travel, cars significantly harm our planet and reduce the attractiveness of walking or using public transport. But guess what? Cutting down on vehicle usage is a brilliant idea! Other countries are already implementing this, proving its feasibility. With fewer cars, our planet, neighborhoods, and public transportation could drastically improve.

Vehicles are severely impacting the environment. Take a look at the article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" by Elisabeth Rosenthal. It details Vauban, Germany's efforts to reduce greenhouse gases. The article notes that cars contribute to 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions. In the US, where driving is prevalent, this figure is even higher, reaching around 50%! This pollution is a major concern, degrading cities. Reducing our dependence on vehicles would significantly decrease these numbers. Governments sometimes intervene to combat vehicle pollution.
