They feared a directly elected leader might become tyrannical, so they ensured the President had to heed Congress. Congress can remove a President for wrongdoing. The Electoral College acts as a safeguard against dictators. Historically, Hitler wasn't elected, and Mussolini gained power through popularity alone. The Electoral College, composed of ordinary citizens, prevents such scenarios. While imperfect, it embodies representative democracy. Richard A. Posner argued it's undemocratic as electors, not the people, vote. All governments are fallible. Despite the U.S. government's good intentions, some want to abolish the Electoral College. Even Nixon, who resigned amid scandal, advocated for its removal. Critics like Bradford Plumer deem it unfair and outdated. However, the Electoral College serves as an election watchdog, ensuring fairness through electors representing each state and its people. This exemplifies representative voting.

The Electoral College represents us all, a product of careful deliberation by America's founders. Despite debates, many great presidents, such as Kennedy, Roosevelt, Lincoln, Washington, and Jefferson, were elected because of it. To safeguard against tyranny and maintain popular sovereignty, the U.S. must preserve the Electoral College.

Look, Senator, this electoral college? It feels outdated. Shouldn't the people directly elect the president? The current system is unjust and flawed. Life is difficult, but a fair nation is vital, especially with the national debt. Obama's policies worsened it, truly. Unequal votes due to location is simply unacceptable.

Electors wield excessive power, diminishing our influence. Though we vote for them, they often disregard our preferences. This disregard erodes trust in government. Shouldn't we, as voters, also have a say on the electoral college? It is highly probable that many citizens concur. Despite my youth, I urge you to listen to the people.

Plumer was correct; we don't have a direct presidential vote. Remember the 2000 election? Gore won the popular vote but lost the presidency. If equality prevails, why do electors receive special treatment? It undermines the significance of our votes. If electors decide, what is the point of voting?

Some argue it prevents a single group from dominating, but that is not how the system operates. The winner-take-all approach within each state dictates the outcome. Who are these electors, and why are they deemed superior? I may be young, but I possess intelligence. Why disregard my vote? Plainly, America is founded on a falsehood, not fairness. We believe we are in control, but elected officials and the electoral college truly govern.

America is in decline, a trend that will continue unless reforms occur. Eliminating the electoral college would offer improvements. Why heed a younger person? Because I understand the frustration of being ignored, regardless of intellect. America understands what is needed. Decisions should not rest solely with a select few. Allow a direct vote for president, at least once. If unsuccessful, revert to the original system. However, stagnation is assured without a trial.

Alright, listen closely – this is important. Here's a changed version, trying to be simpler and more varied, but the same otherwise:

Cars? People usually don't think much of them. But wait! They're REALLY important. We see them every day, but a lot of people can't buy them or even drive them. Look at Vauban, Germany. They said NO to cars! There's a train to Freiburg and roads around, but not inside the town. You *can* have a car, but parking? Forget it. Very little, super expensive. Buying a spot in a garage far away costs $40,000 EXTRA! So, guess what happened? Seventy percent of the people there GOT RID of their cars. More than half SOLD them after moving! Heidrun Walter, a media trainer and mom, said it great: "I was always stressed with a car. This is SO much better." Vauban, since 2006, is showing the future: suburbs WITHOUT cars. Smart idea!

Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," and she was right. Cars are cool, but hello, AIR PROBLEMS! They hurt the environment! Paris got so smoggy, they had to stop drivers for a bit. One Monday? Even license plates STAYED HOME. Or pay about 31 dollars. Next day, maybe odd numbers? The air was SO BAD because the weather held in all the car smoke. Crazy! Traffic went way down – 60% in ONLY FIVE DAYS! The air was like Beijing. When they banned half the cars, the air got better RIGHT AWAY. Odd numbers drove the next day.

Bogota, Colombia, had a NO-CAR DAY. Walk, bike, skate, bus – your choice! No cars AT ALL. You know what that meant? ZERO CRASHES! And this is cool – they do it EVERY YEAR. This was year THREE! They thought seven million people would use something else instead of a car. It rained, but who cared? People still came! Mayor Antanas Mockus said, "Rain didn't stop anyone!" Everyone felt less stressed, and the air was cleaner. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, biked with his wife. "It's good for stress and pollution," he said.

So, in the US, cars are BIG. But…are we done with them? Some think so. We're a huge place with lots of suburbs, so we like cars. Model T, Detroit, "Mustang Sally" – very American. But maybe we're changing our minds. By 2013, driving was down almost 8%. That's like 1995! Money problems didn't help, and fewer people needed to drive to work. But Michael Sivak at Michigan said something else was happening. "Car ownership was going down BEFORE the economy got bad." Wow. We need other ways to travel, so people don't just go back to driving too much. New York's bike rentals, tolls, car rentals? That's good. One study said young people drove 23% LESS between 2001 and 2009!

People want fewer cars for many reasons: they want to be safer, save money, and help the environment.

A lot of folks drive every week. I know I do, with trips to school, sports, and my pals' places. Some say driving's tops – gets us around and helps us do stuff. But driving *too* much? Big problem. Maybe cutting back seems hard, or like it doesn't matter. But hear me out: driving less is fantastic! Cleaner air, healthier bodies, happier feels. That's why the big shots want us to chill on the driving.

Robert Duffer pointed out way back in 2014 that Beijing's air was nasty, all thanks to cars (source 2, paragraph 14). And Paris? Same boat. So they made a rule: drive only on certain days, based on your plates. Break it, get fined (source 2, paragraphs 10-19)! Bam! Air cleaned up quick, so they stopped after just a day. See? Less driving *works*. Elizabeth Rosenthal noted in 2009 that cars muck up the air a ton in Europe and the US (source 1, paragraph 5). Bottom line: drive less for cleaner air and less gas guzzling.

Yeah, driving to work seems easy-peasy. But imagine how much better we'd feel if we drove less! Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, where most families don't even *own* a car (source 1, paragraph 3)! Heidrum Walter lives there car-free and says she's way less stressed (source 1, paragraph 3)! In Bogota, Columbia, Andrew Selsky said they banned cars for a day in 2002 (source 3, paragraph 21). Carlos Arturo Plaza in Bogota felt relaxed and said the air was so much better. Real folks, real proof: less driving, better feels. So, let's do it!

Driving less is so amazing, loads of folks are pushing for it! The EPA's all about supporting communities where people ditch the car. Leaders are making it easier too (source 1, paragraph 9). David Goldberg said we've built everything around cars since WWII, but that's gotta change. Mimi Sheller said change is already happening, and we're seeing a big shift (source 4, paragraph 35)! All this support? It proves less driving has tons of good stuff. So, everybody, let's drive less!

No joke, the world's changing! Young folks are driving less these days (source 4, paragraph 41). Now's the perfect time to ditch the car and grab a bus, bike, or train. Drive less, and we help keep the air sparkling. Plus, we feel happier and healthier! That's why the EPA wants us to chill on the driving. We gotta act now to protect the future. Less driving is just so good for everyone. So, seriously, let's make it happen!

Senator, please listen! I’m a student, so I don’t know tons about politics. Still, I have to say I think we should keep the Electoral College. Doesn't it just make picking a president faster and easier?

There are 538 electors in the Electoral College. To win, you need at least 270 votes. Sure, we technically vote for electors, not the president. But really, we're backing what that candidate stands for. We're voting for a plan. This system makes those swing states super important. That's key, I think. Like the article "In Defense of the Electoral College" says, swing states get way more involved. This could stop elections from dragging on forever and makes sure smaller states still matter.

Lots of states use a "winner-take-all" thing where if you win the state, you get all its electoral votes. There are other ways to do it, like "proportional representation," but I really like the "winner-take-all" setup. Some parts of the Electoral College might seem weird, but this part makes things simpler. It gives all of a state's votes to one person, which keeps things stable. Proportional representation doesn't do that, which could make the election last longer.

Finally, the Electoral College has been around forever. "What Is the Electoral College?" says the founders made it as a middle ground. They didn't want Congress picking the president, but they also didn't want just a popular vote. That history is a big deal. Thanks for reading what I think.

Esteemed State Senator,

I honestly think it's time we said our farewells to the Electoral College and embraced a system where the person with the most votes wins. It's about giving every single one of us a real voice in picking someone who can truly improve our lives. This current setup, where we vote for electors who then pick the president, just feels off. Presidential elections should be open to everyone, not just a select few. The time is now to completely change how we vote.

Why should anyone have to vote for electors who then get to vote for the president? "The Indefensible Electoral College" (source 2) perfectly explains that the Electoral College is rigged, and candidates only focus on states they are barely winning in. But "In Defense of the Electoral College" (source 3) admits that no single area has enough electoral votes to win on its own. This shows how the Electoral College screws over some areas because their votes do not matter, meaning the president can ignore them.

Also, everyone should get to vote, not just citizens. Leaving out so many people just because they aren't citizens doesn’t reflect what the people want. Some areas are at a disadvantage when it comes to electors. This is unfair, as some voices are louder than others. Source 3 says that voters in "tossup" states are more involved because their votes actually count. But it's wrong to only allow "thoughtful voters" to vote when others want to join in, too.

Sure, the Electoral College has some good points. Voting for electors is easy. And if it's a tie, the House of Representatives decides, with each state getting one vote. The system is already in place, with solutions built in. Even though popular vote might have problems, we can fix them. Also, the Electoral College can lead to some areas not having enough electors.

Essentially, the United States should elect its president by popular vote. This could change our society, making everyone feel like they matter and can participate.

Folks all over drive their cars. However, over-reliance on cars brings serious issues. It harms nature and stresses people out. Thus, dialing down car use can make a big difference.

If we eased off on car use, city air could improve significantly. Cars spew nasty pollutants from burning fuel, worsening air pollution. Data reveals that "cars generate 12% of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe and can climb to 50%...in the United States"Source 1. This illustrates cars' big environmental footprint. Smog choked Paris, a city bustling with cars. Reuters noted, "Paris recorded 147 micrograms of particulate matter per cubic meter, versus Brussels' 114 and London's 79.7"Source 2. Temporarily halting cars dropped smog, confirming that car fumes harm the planet.

Cutting car use also dials down community stress. In Vauban, Germany, designed to be car-free, residents enjoy peace. Heidrun Walter shared, "When I had a car I was actually tense. I'm much happier this way"Source 1. This paints a picture of car-free serenity. Bogota, Columbia, saw benefits from a "Day Without Cars". As Andrew Selsky reported, positive changes included "pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks" and "rush hour restrictions have dramatically cut traffic"Source 3. Less driving eases traffic stress.

While helpful, cars cause harm. Their emissions fuel climate change, and jams spark stress. Trimming or axing car use promotes a healthier world.

Dear Senator, I'm writing to voice my concerns about the Electoral College. The way we elect presidents just doesn't sit right with me. While it may have some advantages, I believe the disadvantages far outweigh them. Frankly, I consider it a flawed system that should be abolished.

Although a tie hasn't happened yet, with 538 electors, a deadlock is a real possibility. History shows that even minor shifts in votes could have altered the outcome. According to Richard A. Posner, because 538 is an even number, ties are mathematically possible, albeit rare. It's unsettling that such a small number of votes could lead to a tie. A few flipped votes in Ohio and Hawaii in 1976, for example, could have resulted in a tied election. These numbers may seem significant, but they're insignificant compared to the total number of voters. Despite each state's electors being based on its size, the possibility of a tie remains a concern, one we can avoid by simply counting the popular vote.

Furthermore, electors, while human, can introduce complications. Politics can be messy. Back in 1960, personal disputes nearly led Louisiana to select electors who opposed John F. Kennedy. As Posner points out, parties typically choose electors they trust to vote for their candidate, which is usually the case. Nevertheless, it's possible to win the electoral vote while losing the popular vote. Electors, in theory, can vote as they please, which seems unfair. Voters may feel as if their votes don't matter. While a single vote may not make a difference, candidates should strive to appeal to everyone. Often, they don't bother campaigning in states they know they'll win, since a single region can't elect a president alone. This can lead to voter apathy and disengagement. If the candidate with the most votes wins, people would feel more valued and motivated to vote.

Remember the 2000 election? The popular vote became a contentious issue. It's perplexing when votes in one state determine who gets the electors, which can create problems. When a candidate wins more votes, it benefits them while disadvantaging those who voted for their opponent. Posner discusses issues with runoff elections. Sometimes, a candidate may not win a majority of the votes, which complicates matters and leaves the final decision to the Electoral College.

In conclusion, the Electoral College seems inherently unfair due to the issues it can cause. The US needs a more equitable and just method of electing presidents, where every vote carries equal weight. The current system can lead to ties, broken promises, and disputes over close elections. Switching to a popular vote system would ensure that everyone's voice is heard. Thank you for considering this important matter.

Esteemed Senator, a lot of us believe the Electoral College is super important and should stay. Sure, it's got issues, but it seriously aids voting for both states and the country. The method of voting shows what real people want, based on input from all. So this system makes votes more legit and powerful.

For starters, regular citizens choose who's on the Electoral College. That means people with similar thoughts and feelings can speak up. This helps make certain votes reflect what the general public desires. Every state's voice matters depending on how big it is. These electors? A lot of them get what we need. When picked well, they'll defend the state. Thus, the Electoral College results in much more accurate votes.

Naturally, some argue it has risks, like electors going against what they promised. But that's possible anywhere! Presidents do it too; not all keep promises. Also, the Electoral College needs trust. You've got to trust electors to do the job, just as you trust the president to do what's best. Trust is everything when it comes to voting.

Ultimately, the Electoral College helps make votes way clearer than a simple count. A tie would be really bad! But this system can prevent ties and maybe even help a state or party. Sure, luck can play a part, but that beats a coin flip. The Electoral College gives voting some stability.

In summary, we have to keep the Electoral College. It's better targeted, doesn't need more trust than other votes, and makes the entire thing more sturdy.

Cars turned into the top pick for long hauls, quick. Now, tons drive to work, like, every single day. Folks are getting super lazy, ditching bikes and skates for even tiny trips! Forget walking to the corner store, a crazy amount are just jumping in their cars. This whole driving thing? It's making SO much air pollution. That's a big bummer for our health and heats up the planet, big time. Bottom line: less driving is a total win.

If we ditch the car habit, like, even just a bit, we get cleaner air and a sweet workout bonus.
