Paris, that's usually got smog, is fighting to drop cars too. "Smog was so bad, Paris made rules to block some from driving to get clean air." With these rules, "Traffic got a LOT better in Paris after five smoggy days..." When less people drive, they gotta find other ways to roll, like biking or walking. This keeps them healthy and avoids nasty stuff like cancer, diabetes, and heart issues. Less driving can also fight global warming, stop floods, and keep the ozone layer safe!

"BOGOTA, Colombia—Millions biked, walked, skated, or bussed to work on a car-free day, so streets were empty!" Super fun way to travel and get exercise! Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza said biking with his wife chills him out and helps the air. Professor Mimi Sheller sees this as a big societal shift. The world's changing, and it's hitting our health and the environment hard.

Less driving? Cleaner air, healthier peeps. If we make and drive way fewer cars, folks can bike, skate, or just stroll. NOW is the moment to switch it up. Lots of cities are already on it, and we all gotta jump in, bit by bit.

In American politics, the Electoral College, which decides who becomes president, sparks a lot of intense debate. Many folks, around 60% in some polls, would like to scrap it and go with the popular vote instead. But, there are definitely some loud voices defending the Electoral College. Because it's possible to win the presidency without winning the popular vote, maybe switching to a popular vote would be better.

Each state gets a set number of electoral votes, starting with two, and population decides how many more they get - between three and fifty-five, according to various sites. A candidate needs 270 out of 538 electoral votes to win. Usually, the one who gets the most votes in a state gets all of its electoral votes, which isn't always the fairest deal. Maine and Nebraska, though, do things differently. Some people feel like their vote doesn't count, so they don't bother to vote if their party always loses in their state because of the Electoral College. People who like the Electoral College say it makes sure there's always a clear winner and keeps candidates from only trying to win over one part of the country. Plus, it can still give a result even if the popular vote is super close.

The Electoral College was originally meant to protect smaller states, but it doesn't really do that anymore. Presidential campaigns mostly target swing states to get their electoral votes. Candidates pretty much ignore states that always vote for one party because they already know what will happen. So, if the system isn't working the way it should, why stick with it?

The Electoral College is old and not the best way to pick presidents. It usually finds a winner, but it can still cause a tie. Some say that focusing on swing states makes voters more informed and helps them make better choices because their votes matter more. But, if we got rid of the Electoral College, candidates would just focus their energy somewhere else. The fact that some votes are worth more than others is a problem, so getting rid of the Electoral College is probably the best idea.

Honorable Senator,

There's much chatter regarding the Electoral College's purpose. From the average citizen to powerful politicians, it impacts us all. This system selects our president, striving to make each vote count. When our nation was founded, ensuring fairness between the populace and the government was paramount. Scrapping the Electoral College disrupts this balance. Its existence is essential for a well-functioning government and citizen involvement.

The Electoral College surprisingly benefits the entire nation. Victory necessitates engaging with diverse people in various regions. As highlighted in Source 3, line 19, candidates must "appeal to different regions." Concentrated popularity is insufficient. Maintaining equilibrium prevents regional dominance. This ensures voters are informed about their choices and their significance.

Fairness is the bedrock of our nation's strength. Upholding the Electoral College demonstrates our commitment to integrity. Source 1, line 1, states, "it's a process...the founders made it up to find a middle ground." It was designed to achieve electoral equilibrium. The paramount importance of balance is evident.

Furthermore, the Electoral College generally guarantees a deserving victor based on overall votes. Source 3, line 22, asserts that, "it stops elections where no one gets most of the votes." This straightforward presidential selection method is critical. Concerns about "electors" causing issues are largely unfounded. How can a candidate prevail without substantial backing?

Ultimately, the Electoral College is indispensable for maintaining national equilibrium. We rely on this fairness. Eliminating it would jeopardize our country, its trajectory, and its citizens.

In reality, we should probably ditch the Electoral College. The process is far too convoluted compared to a simple popular vote. It would make much more sense if we just selected the person who received the most votes overall! It's time to admit this Electoral College system is an outdated relic. When electors begin defying the will of the public, it's clear there's a serious issue. While some defend it, the problems it creates far outweigh any potential benefits.

It's needlessly complex. Citizens don't directly elect the President; instead, we choose electors who then make the decision. Should a particular set of electors prevail in a state, their preferred candidate receives all of the state's electoral votes. If the group favored in Florida is inclined for one candidate, imagine them getting ALL 29 of Florida's votes. It creates a serious mess, especially during close races. When there is an electoral tie, the decision goes to the House, where each state gets a single vote. This complicated political theater illustrates why the Electoral College is nothing but a headache. The end result would occur within days rather than months if we only bothered to count all the votes!

The Electoral College system distorts the influence of voters. Since the majority of states award their votes on a winner-take-all basis, candidates prioritize campaigning in a handful of swing states, neglecting the rest of the country. Is this really worth the trouble when a national popular vote is much easier? Discarding the Electoral College would simplify elections and ease tensions by reducing conflict.

Ultimately, the Electoral College just looks unfair, archaic, and foolish. The justifications offered for its preservation are unconvincing, and the arguments against a simple popular vote are even weaker. Keeping the Electoral College introduces unnecessary instability. Shifting to a popular vote system would streamline the presidential election process and likely create a more content populace with less political unrest.

Honorable State Senate Members, I’m urging you to seriously consider a significant change: replacing the Electoral College with a national popular vote for presidential elections. After carefully reviewing how the Electoral College functions, and considering various perspectives, I’ve concluded that maintaining the current system is unacceptable and that adopting a popular vote is undeniably superior.

The Electoral College is complicated. As Posner explains, it deviates from direct democracy. He states that "electors vote for the president, not people... you're actually voting for a slate of electors." This shows how your vote is directed toward individuals already committed to a specific candidate. The Office of the Federal Register clarifies, "The Electoral College is a process, not a place," underscoring that it's defined by its procedures.

Plumer highlights a concerning possibility: "Maybe the scariest thing is if the electoral vote ends in a tie." If a tie occurs, the House of Representatives decides the outcome, but there's a major issue: "Because each state casts only one vote, the single representative from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters." So, a small state wields as much influence as a large state, creating an evident imbalance. The 2000 election, where Gore won the popular vote but Bush won via the Electoral College, illustrates this perfectly. Plumer observes "...the electoral college is unfair to voters...swing states..." Candidates prioritize these states. Plumer concludes, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational," because of potential ties, unfair representation, and fundamental flaws.

Proponents of the Electoral College claim it ensures "...Certainty of Outcome...Everyone's President...Swing States...Big States... Avoid RunOff Elections..." (Posner), but these arguments are weak. The 2000 election lacked certainty. "Everyone's President" isn't accurate, as electors cast votes, not citizens. Swing states receive preferential treatment, disadvantaging others. Posner believes it benefits larger states: "The Electoral College restores some of the weight in the political balance that large states by population lose by virtue of the malapportionment of the Senate decreed in the Constitution..." However, this doesn't rectify the inequity. Posner also asserts that the Electoral College "avoids that problem of elections in which no candidate receives a majority of the votes cast," but the swing state effect compromises vote equality.

In summary, a transition from the Electoral College to a popular vote is essential. The current framework has clear deficiencies, as demonstrated by the 2000 election. The disproportionate influence of swing states and the possibility of ties exacerbate these issues. It's inherently unjust, illogical, and skewed. Despite any dissenting voices, a just, democratic popular vote is the proper solution.

Across the U.S. of A, lots of folks have their own cars; and a whole heap of them reckon they need it just to get from A to B. But hey, there are other ways to travel! And guess what? People are startin' to wanna hit the road less. Sure, it might be a tad tricky, but trust me, it's worth a shot.

Now, cars? Big polluters they are! They're messin' with our health and givin' Mother Earth a headache. Take Paris, for example. They tried blockin' some cars to kick smog to the curb. Get this: according to Source 2, after smog hung around for just five days, traffic went down by a whopping 60%! Even one day with fewer cars did somethin'. Just imagine how much better life could be if we drove less for weeks or even months. No need to ditch the car altogether, but maybe we should pump the brakes on how much we drive.

Drivin' less? It can actually make you feel good, less stressed and all. Sounds kinda strange, right? But other ways of rollin' around can be a whole lot more chill. Take Vauban, Germany. They got rid of almost all the parking spots, driveways, and garages. That way, the shops were close enough to walk to. Heidrun Walter, a media coach and mom, spilled the beans in Source 1, "I was always stressed when I had a car. Now, I am much happier." If a busy mom can swing it, then tons of other folks can too. Bein' cooped up with the family all the time can make you tense. Making it easier and cheaper to walk instead of park? That encourages folks to step outside, meet up with others, and take a breath of that fresh air. Way better than bein' stuck in a car, don't ya think?

Look, drivin' less is a good thing. I'm all in. But to get more people to leave the car keys at home, it's gotta be easier to get to those places that usually need a car to reach.

Automobiles have been the go-to transport option for decades. However, the sheer volume of cars has led to a cascade of issues. A shift away from car dependence would be undeniably beneficial. Such a move could encourage physical activity, reduce air pollution, and unlock substantial savings for individuals and municipalities alike.

A major plus of ditching the car more often is that it gets us moving! Frankly, we've become a bit too sedentary relying on those metal boxes. Cars have edged out walking, biking, and even skating – activities now largely relegated to childhood. Take Vauban, Germany, for instance. With its low car ownership, the streets are alive with the sounds of playing kids and whizzing bikes. This clearly shows that reduced car dependence boosts activity levels. Not to mention Bogota, Columbia, whose "Day Without Cars" initiative proved wildly successful, spurring the creation of over 118 miles of bike lanes in a mere three years. So, the link between less driving and a healthier lifestyle is crystal clear.

Also, dialing back car use would do wonders for our ailing planet. The sheer number of vehicles worldwide makes them a major pollution culprit. Shockingly, in Europe, cars account for about 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions. And, in the United States, that number skyrockets to almost 50 percent! This kind of pollution degrades air quality, especially in densely populated urban areas. Indeed, cars, with few exceptions (cough, power plants, cough), are among the worst polluters out there. So, reduced car use translates directly to a cleaner environment. Simply put: cars are nasty polluters, and curbing their use would be great for Mother Earth.

Finally, lighter car use translates into serious cost savings for both drivers and cities. Maintaining a car – with gas, repairs, and upkeep – really adds up. And, back in April 2013, driving dipped by 9 percent, hitting lows not seen since 1995! The primary reason? Many Americans simply couldn't afford new vehicles. Owning a car has become a major financial burden. On top of that, cities pour vast sums into transportation, but the vast majority goes to highways (80%), while other modes are shamefully underfunded (20%) -- and lawmakers are worried about this unequal balance. It makes sense; less driving really benefits everyone's wallet.

Picture a world with no cars. Imagine streets are totally empty. No traffic, just peace and quiet! Many people would find that way nicer to live. Seriously, less driving would really help the planet. Fewer of those gas-guzzling, speedy cars could seriously help the Earth. Plus, we'd save money and be safer overall.

It's great car companies are trying to make hybrids and cars that don't hurt the Earth as much. But, the biggest thing we can do is get rid of a bunch of cars. Studies show greenhouse gases could drop "...up to 50 percent in some car-heavy spots in the United States." Also, there's more yucky pollution, carbon stuff, and gross smog. Get this: Paris had to ban driving for a bit because of the smog problem! "...Last week Paris had 147 micrograms of PM per cubic meter compared with 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London...". Those numbers are getting scary in Europe and the US. Just think how bad it could be in twenty years if things keep going this way! Fewer cars could really bring those bad levels down. It would stop those gases from messing up the environment and clean the gross air.

Who doesn't love saving cash? Think about saving thousands every year! If you don't drive as much, you can save big by using buses, skipping car insurance, or not having a car at all. Some folks think not having a car is hard. They need to get around, after all. But in Vauban, Germany, there aren't many cars, and people do just fine. Reports state that "Vauban, home to 5,500 residents within a rectangular square mile may be the most advanced experiment in lowcar suburban life". They walk, bike, or take the tram. Now, lots of American cities have carpool apps, buses, and vans. Instead of blowing money on gas, car payments, and insurance, try sharing a ride or getting some exercise!

When you see someone drive away, do you ever worry? Stats show way more people die or get hurt in car crashes than in accidents with other ways to get around. Fewer cars mean less drunk driving, fewer crashes, and fewer people getting hurt or worse. We always hear stories about teens driving drunk and causing terrible accidents. If we use buses and trains, we can stop that and keep our kids safe. Also, traffic can make people stressed and mad, which can lead to more accidents from bad driving. Walking instead of driving is a win-win, giving you fresh air and exercise!

So, cars have pros and cons. They help us get around, but they hurt the environment, drain our wallets, and aren't always safe. By driving less, we help the environment by cutting down on greenhouse gases, pollution, and carbon stuff. We also save tons of money by not paying for car stuff. Less cars overall saves lives by stopping drunk driving and crashes, which means fewer deaths and injuries. Next time you're walking, think about what life would be like without cars.

All over the world, cities are working to reduce the number of cars. You see this in "car-free" zones or driving limits, even "Car-Free Days!" A huge reason? Pollution, especially from those old, smoky diesel trucks. Some places, like Vauban (Germany), Paris (France), and Bogotá (Colombia), are testing things out. And hey, they're seeing positive results, so there's hope!

In Vauban, Germany, you know what's up? Most folks don't even OWN cars. There's no street parking, or car-related stuff, in this "new test area." You can have a car way out on the edge of town, but parking spots? Super rare. If you want one, you buy a garage spot way far away, and it can cost you $40,000!

The truth is, people LOVE not having cars there. They dig hearing "bikes and kids" more than cars roaring by. Vauban is a great "smart plan" in action. In fact, by 2006, most new folks moving in sold off their cars!

Vauban is Germany's 20-year plan to create walkable, bike-friendly suburbs with good bus service. Picture this: roughly 5,500 people crammed into one square mile! It's a leading car-free suburb. Germany's goal? Making car-free life easier. Shops are on main streets, close to houses, not miles away in some highway mall.

Germany is pushing for car-free towns, but some still crave those huge suburbs with garages, like you see outside New York City. Some people still like the old ways, but hey, many are now copying Vauban's cool ideas.

Paris, France, actually stopped some cars to fight air that was as bad as Beijing! The city told drivers with even-numbered license plates to stay home. Break the rule, and it's 22 euros ($31 USD) down the drain. Then, the very next day, odd-numbered plates couldn't drive either.

Guess what? Almost 4,000 cars were towed! But traffic? It fell by almost 60%! French leaders pointed the finger at diesel cars, despite them having a tax break. Guess what? 67% of French cars run on diesel.

Paris has some of the worst air in Europe because of diesel cars. This driving ban made things tough on some, like delivery trucks. But there were good things too! Electric cars, hybrids, and cars with 3+ people were allowed. Also, public transit was free. Air quality improved, and the ban was lifted in just days.

Bogotá, Colombia, started "Car-Free Day," hoping other places would copy. Millions walked, skated, biked, or hopped on the bus to work. Colombia does this one day a year, for three years running. Driving will cost you $25 if you disobey. The city, with its 7 million people, wants less smog and more folks using other ways to get around.

Despite the rain, more people showed up than anyone thought! Two more Colombian cities joined in for the first time ever! Officials from other countries checked out Bogotá. They were pumped and said Colombians were changing their ways, and thought this trend would catch on!

This whole thing started in the mid-1990s. Bogotá built 118 miles of bike paths – the most in Latin America. They built parks and sports areas too. Tiny sidewalks became wide paths. New places to eat and shop opened up because of Car-Free Day.

Studies show Americans are buying fewer cars and driving less. This lines up with Obama's goals. The US buys tons of cars, but people might not love them as much anymore because of the pollution.

One reason might be the recession. Many Americans couldn't swing buying new cars, and that's still happening. So, the big car companies are struggling to stay afloat.

Ford and Mercedes are changing and calling themselves "mobility companies." They're making all sorts of different stuff. Young people (16 to 39) are getting fewer licenses. Loads of them share rides, walk, or take the bus. They just don't think they need a license.

Tons of countries sometimes stop driving to fight pollution. And? They see cleaner air. These countries tackle major problems that cause bigger issues, like global warming. Things are changing, and that helps everyone!

Senator, history notwithstanding, I still think the Electoral College is the best way we can pick our leaders. It’s got some really important stuff going for it. Like, for starters, it makes those swing states super important, and the way it gives out votes matches how many people live in each state. Plus, it keeps someone from winning just by being popular in one tiny area.

Because the Electoral College exists, swing states become make-or-break, which forces everyone to think a lot more carefully. People there know their votes actually count, so they really try to figure out who they should be backing. This makes it more likely the best candidate will actually win. Without that winner-take-all thing going on, those swing states wouldn't matter nearly as much if we just went by who got the most votes overall.

The way the Electoral College doles out votes, based on how big each state is, is also key. If you want to be president, you go after those bigger states because they've got more electoral votes up for grabs. But if we went by popular vote, candidates might just skip the big states and try to win over all the little ones instead. Our system keeps things on an even keel, so smaller states don’t get too much say.

On top of all that, the Electoral College stops someone from winning just because they're super popular in one area. They have to go all over the place and try to win people over, even in places that might not like them so much. If we switched to a popular vote, campaigns would be all over the place, because they wouldn’t know where to focus since the winner-take-all thing would be gone. The Electoral College makes it tough for someone who’s only popular in one region to win since there would be no longer a real need to focus elsewhere.

Basically, that's why I think the Electoral College beats a popular vote when it comes to electing presidents. And those are the main things that make it better, in my book.

The Electoral College feels incredibly dated when it's time to elect presidents, doesn't it? Why are we *still* clinging to it after all these years? America *must* evolve; the candidate with the most individual votes should simply win.

Sure, the winner-take-all approach *sounds* straightforward, but is it genuinely just? As stated by the Federal Register, most states operate like this: secure the state, and you take all of its electoral votes. Some argue that dedicating significant effort to a state warrants claiming all its votes, even with a *narrow* victory. It's a high-stakes gamble, wouldn't you agree? That's the justification for maintaining it. And what about article one? Each candidate has electors, selected by their *party*, who are the ones who cast the actual votes. So, we're voting for *them* so they can vote for the candidate? That's absurd. People should have a direct say! The Electoral College must end immediately.

Bradford Plumer's piece, "The Indefensible Electoral College," presents a compelling case. We don't *directly* vote for the president, but rather for electors, and *they* are the ones who decide who the president is. Every single vote *must* carry the same weight! Giving some votes more influence based on where you live? That's unfair! Plumer illustrates, "If you lived in Texas... you'd vote for a group of 34 Democratic electors committed to Kerry.” Why should Kerry receive 34 votes solely because his party nominated those individuals? He should only get votes from individuals who *chose* him! If they don't support him, their vote shouldn't count. All votes must be equal; it's time to scrap it.

Plumer also raises a troubling prospect: what happens in the event of a tie? What then? The House makes the decision, with each *state* getting only one vote. Is that really fair? Wyoming, a *small* state, gets the same level of input as California, which is home to *millions* of people? That’s far from equitable! With a popular vote, ties become *nearly* impossible. Also, Plumer notes, "Al Gore... won the popular vote but lost the presidency." The candidate preferred by the majority didn't win because of the *Electoral College*! Bush became president instead. It's a flawed system that creates far more problems than solutions.

We urgently need a popular vote, without delay. It’s the better option for all and is much fairer for candidates. The Electoral College must be abolished, once and for all.

It's no secret that cars are basically essential these days – you can't imagine life without them, right? But while they make life simpler, we tend to overlook the downsides of relying so heavily on them. So, this piece will explore the consequences of car dependence, laying out both the issues and potential solutions.

A major concern is the contribution of cars to global greenhouse gas emissions. Did you know that in Europe, cars account for roughly 12% of these emissions? Vauban, a town in Germany, is taking an ambitious approach to address this. Their "car-free" vision necessitates significant changes. Increasingly, communities are drawn to this concept, envisioning compact, interconnected neighborhoods with efficient public transit options. Heidrun Walter, a resident, remarked, "With a car, I was always stressed. This is a happier life." While some might find the shift challenging, others experience definite improvements.

Smog, a significant issue in urban areas, is yet another consequence of car usage. Paris faced such severe pollution that driving restrictions were implemented to improve air quality. The subsidized cost of diesel fuel in France only exacerbated the smog, highlighting the need for regulatory reform. However, fresh statistics indicate a decline in car purchases, licensing, and driving among Americans. Considering America's substantial pollution contribution, this trend could benefit the environment. The internet is also playing a role, as young people connect online, reducing the necessity for travel. As Bill Ford of Ford Motor Company aptly put it: "Pedestrians, bicycles, cars, and public transport can all work together in a connected system to save time, use less stuff, make less pollution, and be safer."

Bogota, Colombia, is actively encouraging alternative transportation methods to combat smog. According to Carlos Arturo, a local businessman, "It's a good chance to get rid of stress and make the air cleaner." The city has invested in more recreational areas, parks, improved sidewalks, congestion pricing, and appealing retail spaces. This initiative is attracting international attention.

These are just a few examples of the benefits that arise from reducing car dependence. These initial indicators suggest that greater improvements are possible as more individuals and communities embrace alternatives to car-centric lifestyles.

The process for selecting our U.S. president involves citizens voting for their preferred candidate. However, sometimes, the candidate who receives the most votes does not win! We instead utilize a system called the Electoral College. Essentially, winning a state means securing all of its allocated "electoral votes." Afterward, a group of electors cast these votes. I personally dislike this system, and many agree. It compromises representation, diminishes voter participation, and compels candidates to focus solely on specific states.

The Electoral College has one significant flaw. It's simply unjust. States with larger populations possess more electoral votes. Wyoming, for instance, with a limited population of around 500,000 voters, receives very few electoral votes. Consequently, each voter's individual say is somewhat undervalued in the pursuit of reaching 270 electoral votes. It's simply unfair. Furthermore, the candidate who receives the most total votes can still lose the election, remember Bush v. Gore in 2000?

Certain individuals contend that distributing electoral votes based on state population is fair. However, a national popular vote would be more fair. The candidate who receives the most votes nationally wins, that's it. In states where one party dominates, like Republicans in Texas, that party consistently wins. As a result, a Democrat in Texas might feel their vote is inconsequential, leading them to not vote. That's bad for voters and contradicts the American values.

As a state's population increases, its influence in the Electoral College also grows. This prompts candidates to focus on these "big states," where they engage with voters. States with less population? Frequently overlooked. Candidates travel extensively, giving speeches, while ignoring certain states. In 2000, candidates ignored seventeen states! As a result, many residents in these states stop voting.

The Electoral College fails. It alienates voters, dampens voter turnout, and causes candidates to focus on states with larger populations. A transition to a national popular vote would solve these problems, and vastly improve presidential elections.

Esteemed Senator,

I am writing to express my concern about the Electoral College (EC), highlighting both its advantages and disadvantages. This system determines our president and vice president, yet many Americans question its fairness. Critics argue that it is outdated and unjust, suggesting a simple popular vote as a replacement. While the Electoral College might seem frustrating because it doesn't let people directly pick the president, it does offer certain benefits. For instance, it helps correct issues and makes voting easier. The college also lowers the total votes needed for a clear victory. It also tries to ensure representation from all over the US. However, it's essential to ask whether these positives outweigh the negatives. Is ease of use a good enough justification for separating the public from direct presidential elections? Some view the Electoral College as a helpful "safety net" to streamline elections. However, many Americans disagree, seeing it as a danger to the core principles of the US. Consequently, it's clear that the Electoral College needs reform.

The idea of the EC as a voting safeguard raises worries. While it sounds good in theory, it often results in skewed representation. Citizens vote for party electors, who are expected to support their party's candidate. Richard Posner, in "In Defense of the Electoral College," mentions that the Electoral College winner might not always secure the popular vote. He stresses that this is relatively uncommon. Posner's point is valid, as it doesn't happen very often. The EC mainly operates on a "winner-take-all" basis, where the candidate with the most votes in a state gets all of that state's electoral votes. This raises ethical problems. The president should represent the majority, but someone will always be dissatisfied. The winner should get the most votes to genuinely represent Americans. Smart voters learn about each candidate and decide who is best. Voting is them voicing their opinion about who would be a good president. If most people choose someone, it makes sense for that person to win. However, the EC gives all state electoral votes to the candidate with the most electors in that state. Thus, some people who would be fairly represented by a popular vote may not get real representation. This gap hurts the country, and even the president.

A major concern with the Electoral College is that it prevents Americans from directly choosing their president. Supporters argue that "the EC avoids the problems of polling every American." They claim that polling everyone has issues like too many votes, making counting difficult. However, ensuring accurate votes is the president's duty. Ultimately, they must represent those who voted for them by empowering them to pick their president. In this case, "practicality" is simply their excuse to stop Americans from voting. A popular vote might contain errors, but the Electoral College has fewer electors so less errors. The EC aims to mirror the population, ensuring the candidate is good for many in an area, not just one area. Despite its efforts, the EC isn't perfect. Posner states, "the most thoughtful voters should decide the election," and he makes a valid point. An uneducated voter is a danger to any nation. Yet, the US promises every citizen the right to vote. No one in power can arbitrarily declare "only the educated should decide," as defining an educated voter is not easy.

The EC's problems are worsened by the influence of large states. More populous states have more people, so a candidate chosen by popular vote represents those people, no matter where they live. Smaller states argue they should have an equal say in the Senate and EC. Each state got two senators, no matter the size, because of the Senate. However, the Electoral College doesn't give equal representation to smaller states. A candidate's win in a popular vote depends on the state's population. The EC also depends on the state's population. Since smaller states don't have many electoral votes, they want equal votes in the US Senate. Sadly, the EC doesn't do that.

Knowing where this comes from is key to making real change. Accuracy should be a goal if possible, but aimed for even if thought impossible. Keeping the EC as it is lacks accuracy, and offers representation only for ease. A good Electoral College should see itself as helping, not fighting, popular votes. I must state my disappointment with the EC's current state. I know some things can't be changed, but getting the best method is crucial. Senator, you were voted in. We trusted you to defend our needs. We want you to represent us when picking a president. Because of what I've said, we want the Electoral College removed for a better voting system.

Okay, cars are good for long trips, but there are more ways to travel nearby like buses or bikes. Less car usage has huge perks. Cars are easy, sure, but cheaper travel exists!

For instance, trains and buses can move us together or alone. These choices save power, lower pollution, and boost travel safety. Cars crash when drivers get tired or can't find parking. Also, cars cost a lot. Gas and fixes never end. When cash is low, subways or buses cost less. Getting your car towed makes things worse, really. When air is still, exhaust sits, making pollution worse too. Some claim they need cars to live, but public options say no.

Also, less car use aids Earth. Roads might look packed, but less driving cuts smog. Car-free plans show good things when we drive less, like less stress and cleaner air. That makes a big change. We get more parks, nice walks, less traffic, and cool new shops. While the U.S. loves cars, new issues might stop constant driving, especially with these good changes!

So, owning a car is huge, and while they help sometimes, we can live without them sometimes too. Of course, cars matter, but we must think about Earth. Cars are simple, but there are more ways to travel without spending so much.

That ceaseless cacophony – car alarms screaming, horns blaring, engines roaring? It's the soundtrack to our lives now, the daily grind's anthem. Truth be told, way too many cars clutter our streets!

Seriously, curtailing our reliance on driving? It's a must.

Ponder the possibilities, the sheer benefits that would unfold!

Cars appear essential, yet often, they're anything but. Sure, cross-country treks demand them. But a quick jaunt to the corner store? Please!

Driving less: paramount. Families hemorrhage cash weekly on gasoline – a staggering $20 to even $50! Less driving, bigger savings, meaning more money for necessities and joyful family moments.

"Owning a car was pure stress. Life is simply better without," shared one individual. Car-free folks? Consistently happier, it seems.

And, a bonus: cleaner air! Better health for all!

"It reduces stress and purifies the air," states Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, pedaling alongside his wife. People minimizing driving? They're generally thrilled about it.

If more understood the splendor of minimized driving and embraced it, cleaner air would abound, and an uplifted spirit would follow.

One former driver noted her children "...plan their summers around walking, buses, or shared rides." Plus, there's no license-acquisition frenzy, despite cars having their uses. Some even lean towards walking instead of driving.

Driving isn't inherently evil, but less driving? Undeniably beneficial. Savings accumulate while walking, air quality elevates for everyone, and focus realigns: work, loved ones, family.

Try it! Park that car, hide those keys! Walking provides a surprisingly fun shift from the norm.

Please, Senator, hear me out – as a student, I'm imploring you to end the Electoral College. A national popular vote for president is a must! Think of it: every vote would actually count, unlike the strange, indirect method we're stuck with now.

Honestly, with the current setup, we're not even directly electing the president! These electors, chosen by state parties, diminish our influence. We need a more significant role in deciding our leader.

Remember back in the '60s when Louisiana almost botched their elector selection? That near miss highlighted how easily the Electoral College can be compromised. It could have resulted in complete turmoil! Maintaining this system is practically inviting future problems.

And get this: the "winner-take-all" approach distorts campaigns, too. Candidates only prioritize swing states, disregarding countless individuals in states they deem secure wins or losses.

The Electoral College is outdated and flawed. It undermines direct representation, courts potential crises, and skews elections with its absurd "winner-take-all" rule. We deserve a transparent system, one that averts past errors and rectifies the laughable "winner-take-all" issue.

The electoral college, using electors as go-betweens for voters and presidential hopefuls, is clearly not perfect. Instead of just directly electing the president, people pick others to make the final call, which hides what their votes really mean. Wouldn't simply adding up all the individual votes be easier to grasp? Honestly, the electoral college just isn't living up to democratic standards.

It's supposed to even the playing field for smaller states, yet the big ones still call the shots. Some states have loads of electoral votes, others hardly any. Still, if small states band together, they can have more clout than the big ones. Plus, a candidate can win the popular vote but lose the election if their supporters are mostly in certain areas. So, the electoral college can mess up the overall count with its elector system.

Figuring out how the popular vote and electoral votes differ can be a real head-scratcher. A candidate might do great in California, snagging tons of electoral votes. But doing equally well in Montana gets them way fewer. Even though they put in the same effort in both states, the electoral vote system favors candidates who dominate in states with big populations.

Making matters worse, the electoral college is a winner-takes-all game. Candidates focus on campaigning in states like California and New York because winning, even by a hair, gets them all of that state's electoral votes. Even if it's a close race, like 49% to 51%, the loser gets zero electoral votes. A better system would split up electoral votes based on how people actually voted.

To wrap it up, the electoral college is nowhere near perfect. A simple popular vote would be clear, fair, and easy to understand, no confusing explanations needed.

Honestly, our nation is facing some serious issues right now. The environment is suffering, and many individuals are struggling financially. We need immediate action, like, yesterday! Here's a thought: let's reduce driving! This could potentially address both problems simultaneously. Fewer cars mean cleaner air, which benefits the planet. Additionally, people could save a lot of money, easing their financial burdens. In essence, less driving equals a major victory.

Our environment is being severely damaged by excessive pollution. According to Robert Duffer, Paris, France, occasionally experiences smog and pollution comparable to Beijing, China! Consequently, the French government implemented strict driving restrictions and imposed fines on violators. The smog cleared up as a result! However, they eventually lifted the regulations. Nonetheless, there's a lesson here for polluted areas: regulations like these can effectively improve air quality and safeguard public health.

If you're short on money, cutting back on driving can be a real game-changer. The money you save on car expenses can be used for essential needs. Elisabeth Rosenthal mentioned towns in Germany where, incredibly, 70% of residents don't even own cars! The reason? Parking is extremely difficult and parking spots can cost up to $40,000. Therefore, abandoning the car is much more economical. Some individuals sold their cars simply to relocate there. Rosenthal's message is clear: living car-free can save you considerable money, especially if you're in financial trouble.

Really, there are many benefits to driving less. Cleaner air contributes to a healthier environment, which is beneficial for everyone and the entire planet. Moreover, you save money, which is great if you're broke. Some might complain that it complicates commuting to work, school, or other destinations. But there are alternatives available! Bicycles, buses, trains, even monorails! Consider the consequences of excessive driving: significant pollution and exorbitant expenses on gas and car maintenance. By reducing driving, we eliminate these problems.

Senator, listen up!

We have got to trash the Electoral College, and fast! Yes, it's been around since the Constitution was written, but it's got to go. Every person's vote should matter. The Electoral College is not fair. It treats states badly, too! We want real freedom and equality, and this system only holds us back.

The U.S. says it's about rights for everyone. But is that true when people can't vote directly for the president? You're voting for electors who then pick the president according to the Office of the Federal Register. So, what's the point of even voting? It's a problem when electors ignore what the people want. Bradford Plumer talked about how Al Gore "won the vote but lost the presidency" in 2000 because of this system. Then, a Gallup poll found that more than 60 percent of voters wanted a simple, direct vote. Also, in 1960, someone tried to change the electors in Louisiana against John F. Kennedy. Someone was trying to mess with the election! It doesn't happen a lot, but how long before it's constant? Americans should pick their leaders, end of discussion.

Also, the Electoral College makes the U.S. unequal because it treats states differently. The winner-takes-all system means candidates only care about a few states – the big ones, swing states, or states they care about. Bradford Plumer said that "during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all." It's all twisted. How can people choose when candidates don't even talk to them? Equality means a fair vote for everyone, no exceptions.

In short, we have to scrap the Electoral College because it’s not fair to people or states. Getting rid of it will help the U.S. actually live up to what it says it believes in.

Sincerely,

A U.S. Citizen.

The world shapes our habits, and we keep making pollution, so we’re trying harder to stop it. Many countries are helping, not just the U.S., but also Colombia, France, and Germany. Some Germans dumped their cars and feel much happier! A Colombian city has a car-free day each year—a nice break from stress and pollution, but breaking the rules costs you. Could driving less make life better, do you think?

Vauban, a German town built by 2006 to nix cars, totally worked! About 70% of homes there are car-free, and over half sold their cars to move there. Almost no cars exist there, no street parking, garages, or driveways. A tram runs right to the city down the main street. More places like Vauban are popping up in Europe, which says a lot. The EPA wants car-lite spots here in the U.S., and some politicians are trying. This is "smart planning," with closer cities, easy walking, and less need to drive to the store.

Paris had awful pollution, so a law stopped driving on certain days to clean the air. One Monday, odd-numbered license plates were banned. Drive, and you pay! The next day, even numbers were out. Almost 4,000 got fined, and 27 cars got towed; people were mad. Still, there were 30% fewer cars, even with all the fuss!

Bogota, Colombia, started a car-free day, and it’s a big deal now. More folks walk or bike, and the air's cleaner. Only buses and taxis can drive. Millions skate, bike, hike, or walk. They're always out there, rain or shine. They say it's great for relaxing and breathing clean air. Valledupar and Cali, also in Colombia, are doing the same. Even the mayor of Asuncion, Paraguay, came to watch. The city has added bike paths, new sidewalks, sports spots, and parks to make walking better.

See why so many countries are working hard to save the planet? Car-free days and zones really help. Maybe I’ve made you think about living more and driving less.

State Senator, I'm really concerned about the Electoral College. It's super old and feels really off to me, like it doesn't make sense. I wish it would just go away because voters lose out, we could have a tie – and here's a shocker – we actually vote for electors, not the president himself!

The Electoral College does voters dirty. Candidates only pay attention to swing states. These states get ALL the votes to the winner, so they ignore the others. Its like they don't exist! Back in 2000, candidates ignored like seventeen states! Big media markets also got ignored with no ads either. Voters don't get enough information in these states! Candidates hide things! Without ads or visits, voters can't really know who they're voting for, making the Electoral College unfair.

Can you imagine an Electoral College tie? It'd be a total disaster. If that happens, the House decides, each state getting one vote. Seriously? The result might not even be what the people in those states want. It relies on a few voters and that's not good. A tie would ruin the election.

Also, the real connection between us and the president gets all messed up. We don't vote directly for the president; we vote for electors instead. So, say you're in Texas and you like John Kerry? You're voting for 34 Democratic electors who said they'd vote for him. Plus, anyone can be an elector. Someone who isn't involved in politics might be biased. The Electoral College is messed up.

Mr. Senator, the Electoral College is just no good. Voters get the short end of the stick, we could end up with a tie, and the election isn't even direct. It feels wrong to voters. The tie possibility sucks, and we are voting for an elector, not the president himself. I just don't think it's right, it's old-fashioned, and it's confusing.

Thinking of giving up the car? Smart move! You'll help the earth, save cash, and skip the awful traffic.

A big plus? You'll be helping the planet. Cars pump out lots of greenhouse gases, as Elizabeth Rosenthal notes. Europe is at 12%, but car-heavy places like the US can hit 50%! That gross exhaust makes smog, especially in cities. Robert Duffer talks about Paris trying to get rid of cars to fight smog. Some think about suburbs with less need for cars. Less cars means cleaner air, plain and simple.

Also, fewer cars mean less traffic! Bogota, Colombia had a "no car day" to get clean air and open roads. Less traffic meant less stress, plus more moving around. No more late arrivals! People walked, biked, or bussed. As a bonus, Bogota added parks and sports spots. Fewer cars are good for health and the earth.

Lastly, you'll save money! You'll buy less gas and spend less on car stuff. You'll also pay less in taxes for roads, parking, and lights. Less stuff will be needed for roads and parking areas. Since many city folks already use the subway or bus to save money, cutting back on cars is an easy way to cut pollution.

Basically, driving less is a big win all around. It helps the earth, cuts traffic, makes you more active, and saves you money. It might be tougher in spread-out areas, but it's great in cities!

Okay, cars, right? Seriously, they're everywhere! All over the U.S., all over the world. People need them, for jobs, for family, for just getting around, you know? But get this: car-free living is becoming a real thing in Europe, the U.S., and other places! Like Vauban, a town in Germany, where they pretty much said no to cars. Heidrun Walter, a mom and media person there, said, "I was stressed with a car, now I'm way happier.” So, it works, and it makes you feel good!

It's a challenge, for sure, but the good things are huge. Cars make, like, 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, and maybe even half in the U.S., a report says! Driving less means less pollution, which is a big deal. Remember Paris? They banned cars because of smog, letting them in only on certain days based on their plates. Traffic went down 60%, and the air got cleaner! With changes like this, you gotta wonder: what if cities were car-free all the time? The benefits could be massive!

Car-free doesn’t have to be forever. Ever heard of Bogota, Colombia? Every year, they have a car-free day, super popular, with millions participating, according to a report. For three years, they stopped cars (except buses and taxis) to get people to try other ways to get around and to stop smog in the city of seven million people. People who broke the rules got fined! Plus, Cali and Valledupar joined in for the first time! A businessman, Carlos Arturo Plaza, said, "It's a great time to relax and clean the air." So, even a little car-free time can make a big difference!

It's wild, but the U.S. might be using cars less and less, another report states. Studies show Americans aren't buying as many cars, aren't driving as much, and fewer people are getting licenses each year. Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives (an investment research company) found that we drove the most in 2005, and it's been going down since then. By April 2013, driving had gone down by almost 9% from the peak. So, Americans are relying less on cars! The article says this could really lower carbon emissions and help the environment. Experts think this will continue, with more alternative transportation coming out. New York City has bike sharing; San Francisco has BART. There are tons of options!

Basically, car-free living and other ways to get around are awesome! With plenty of ways to drive less, green travel is easier than you think. So many choices exist, and studies show that the good things are bigger than the bad.

Senator Adams,

The presidential election? Complicated! The Electoral College means we pick electors instead of voting straight for the top dog. Parties choose these electors, who might not do as we want. You can get the most votes but still not win due to the electoral vote count. Voters are unsure and don't understand this process. Feels old, needs fixing.

Electors are questionable, which is a huge worry. Parties pick them, and states have their own rules. Voters barely influence elector choices. Counting popular votes makes sense. "Electors might ditch what people really want," so make it reliable! Electors have too much influence.

You can get more votes but lose if you miss the needed electoral votes. Voters are bummed, electors decide it all. "Gore beat Bush in votes, but not the electoral votes?" It happens! Another close race, most votes might lose. That's bad.

Folks are lost about the Electoral College. An article noted, "voters mixing up electors mess up their vote." This system doesn't show who we are voting for. Someone shared how people voted for electors but were unsure. Voters vote and may be confused.

Let's trash the Electoral College and use the popular vote. Because of their party love, electors aren't always trusted. A popular vote win might lose due to lacking electoral votes. It's time for a big change. People are totally confused.

Dear Senator,

This Electoral College? Still stirring up dust, even now. Wouldn't it be grand to just say goodbye? Right now, it's all roundabout – we choose electors, and *they* choose the prez. Change their minds? Ha! They might do whatever they like! Just think, one vote could rewrite history. People crave a fix. After the 2000 uproar – Gore's win in the popular vote, but loss of the election – polls showed that most Americans prefer the direct vote.

Granted, the Bush v. Kerry showdown is rare, but it *has* happened. Do we really need another one to drive the point home? We're all for democracy, right? Power to the people? But *do* we really have the power? It might seem so, but the Electoral College – a mere 538 individuals – wields the true influence. It is fundamentally flawed. It's ripe for manipulation, unfairly favoring one side. In 1960, a sneaky scheme almost ousted the Democratic electors in Louisiana to thwart Kennedy. Chilling!

And those electoral votes? Not fair, not at all. Picture the House, where bigger states get a bigger slice of the elector pie. California? 55 votes, a giant's share compared to South Dakota or Alaska. The Senate adds balance in Congress, but there's no such balance here. The giants always triumph! And what if the votes are tied? The House decides, each state getting a *single* vote. Wild, right?

In conclusion, the Electoral College is ancient, unjust, and dysfunctional. We could simply vote for the president directly, no electors needed. It's undemocratic, and it's time for it to vanish.

Sincerely.

Alright, so the Electoral College? Forget bricks and mortar. Think back when the country first began. The founders put it in the Constitution way back when. They weren't keen on just Congress picking the President. But, they also felt iffy about *everyone* just voting. So, *electors* were given the job of casting votes. Congress then tallies them up. Ancient history, right? Is it really fair? Does your vote even matter that much? Some say electors think things through really hard. However, many think their voice is limited. Maybe a change is needed?

So, voting doesn't *really* choose the president. Instead, you choose *electors*! These electors then pick the president. So they decide it, even if the popular vote goes somewhere else! Think of it as a swing vote. You might win the people's vote, but actually lose. Because the electors didn't vote for you. It's happened before, right? Remember 2000? Gore won the popular vote, but lost because of the Electoral College. Some say leave it because it's tradition. But what about other options?

Remember, the Electoral College comes from a long time ago. But, it can mess things up! The people's pick can be ignored. You can win the vote, but lose the election. Maybe a new system is better. One with everyone included, not just the electors.

Because it's so old, the Electoral College has issues, alright. It needs fixing, not ignoring. Your vote feels like it doesn't matter, right? So, fix the Electoral College! Otherwise, what's the point of voting anyway?

Dear Senator, I've been giving how we elect the President a lot of thought lately. This Electoral College thing? I'm honestly not convinced. I understand why the founders put it in the Constitution back when the country was just starting out. But now that we all know "The Electoral College consists of 538 electors," is this really the best system we have today? Wouldn't a simple popular vote for the President be easier for everyone? The Office of the Federal Register states that "The Electoral College consists of the selection of the electors," and with "a majority of 270 electoral votes is required to elect the President," does it really feel like the people are being heard? Surely, with all the intelligent people around, we can come up with a better, more direct way to choose our leader?

It's a tough situation, definitely, but you, Senator, could help improve it. Since "electors are generally chosen by the candidate's political party," shouldn't we ensure regular citizens have a stronger say? And given that "the presidential election is held every four years," Americans have to wait a long time to make their voices heard. Why not give them a direct vote, the kind they actually desire? As it stands, "most states have a winner-takes-all system that awards all electors to the winning presidential candidate. However, Maine and Nebraska each have a variation of proportional representation." We could avoid all this complexity and simply do what the people want: allow them to directly vote for their President. Isn't that what America truly represents, Senator?

In fact, "according to a gallop poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gorethanks to the quirks of the electoral collegewon the popular vote but lost the presidency, over 60% of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now." And as Bradford Plumer recently pointed out, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational. The best arguments in favor of it are mostly assertions without much basis in reality. And the arguments against direct elections are spurious at best."

Down here in Florida where the sun shines bright, the Electoral College? I'm a fan. Honestly, it just *feels* correct, doesn't it? It definitely bumps up the chances we'll get a president most people are actually cool with. Because – get this – it makes elections cleaner, gets us all behind one person, and *really* matters in those swing states.

Now, yeah, the Electoral College ain't perfect, but swapping it for a simple popular vote? Might blow up in our faces! How so, you ask? Well, usually it hands out a bigger win than just the popular vote would. And! It throws a little love to the smaller states too. Even with those 538 electors, a tie? Super rare. And the winner? They HAVE to be fair to everyone. No picking favorites – no matter what – religion, region, nothing. If not, people feel ignored, like their vote is trash, and the president just doesn't care.

You see those presidential hopefuls mostly chilling in swing states? There's a reason! People say those states have the "smartest" voters, so *they're* the ones who should be calling the shots, get it?

People complain that the Electoral College is handing too much power to the government. But you know what? Without it, we're even MORE likely to see that! The Electoral College kind of gives the power back to US, the voters, so we can pick our president.

In short? Keeping the Electoral College allows us regular folks to carefully choose who's going to lead us for the next four years.

The Electoral College, a really old system for picking presidents, is super important. We should keep it because it spreads power out evenly across the states, and we usually get a clear winner. Plus, elections stay on schedule. Over time, it's picked good leaders who know what we want. We, the people, get to pick, and the Electoral College keeps things smooth.

Think of it this way: big states, like Florida, get more votes (like 29!), while small states, like Wyoming, get fewer (only 3). It's like how states get senators and representatives. Because of population, every state gets at least three votes, which makes sense. This stops big states from bossing everyone around. If Wyoming's population jumped, they might get more votes, and Florida's could drop! Florida has tons of different people, so more votes make sense. The Electoral College also makes people think before voting. Without it, a candidate from California could win just because of California's many voters.

Okay, ties can happen, but they almost never do because most states have a "winner-take-all" thing. Swing states are key! Candidates know not to waste time in states that always vote for the other side. Instead, they focus on undecided states. They also count on their usual states. It's like shopping: you buy what you need, not what you have or can't use. So, don't complain about where candidates go!

Our presidential inaugurations are always on January 20th or March 4th; let's keep it that way! Sticking to the schedule matters a lot, and it's harder without the Electoral College. It keeps elections smooth and on time, with voting always on the second Thursday of November. If we got rid of the Electoral College and just counted all votes, it could mess things up. Imagine recounting all the votes in Texas – it could take months! Then we'd be without a leader, causing major problems.

The Electoral College has been a big part of American democracy forever, and things could be tricky without it. Traditions are good, and this is one of them. It spreads power, ensures a clear winner, and keeps our election schedule on track. It brings order, and we all benefit from the structure it provides.

Drive less. Save money, time, and frustration. At almost fifteen, getting my permit can wait. Cars are not something I need right now. The mall and restaurants are close, so why drive? Imagine fewer wrecks and less money spent on gas if others drove less!

After a bad air day, Paris pushed greener travel options. Just think, car-free days in every country! Imagine less traffic, cleaner air, and safer streets. Bus rides and leisurely walks could be the norm on those days! It's a great chance to fix the air quality and reduce wrecks. Post-WWII, cities were designed around cars. It is time to rethink urban planning! Focusing on things that make life better should be the new priority. Cars may be a fast way to travel, but they cause pollution! We need clean air! Maybe driving less will do the trick. "Old bills favored highways, with 80% going to them and 20% to other transport".

Car costs can be a huge burden. Instead, that money could fund a dream vacation or your kids' college! Is going broke over a car really worth it? Why stress when you could be enjoying life? Cars often bring financial pressure and more problems than solutions. Last year, a study showed a 23% drop in young drivers from 2001 to 2009! Maybe young people are seeing the drawbacks of driving. A license can mean chores like grocery runs and kid-shuttling. Plus, gas, wrecks, tickets, and tolls add up. The many issues of cars lead to fewer people driving and more people riding the bus or walking.

My aunt gripes about her car but drives to Walmart when she could walk! Always choose to drive less. Cleaner air and safer streets are the goal. It's much better than polluted air and car expenses. Driving less helps everyone!

Senator, the Electoral College has caused quite a stir, with folks questioning its very existence! While some staunchly defend its current form, a significant number cry out for reform. So, what's the game plan? Personally, I think it needs a major overhaul. As it stands, the system seems to prioritize vote tally over the voters themselves. It is also a bit odd we don't vote directly for the top job, and those electors can even buck the people's will. And consider this: one state, or even a lone elector, can swing the whole election! Frankly, that makes our democracy feel a bit shaky.

Despite the defenses it may have, the Electoral College brings a host of issues. Candidates tend to zoom in on the states with the biggest electoral prize, potentially overlooking those with smaller populations. Like, a candidate might spend weeks in California, chasing those 55 electoral votes, while giving Montana, with its measly 3, the cold shoulder. It feels more like a hunt for electoral victories than a genuine conversation with everyone. Seems like the election is just about charming a few big states instead of representing all of us.

As "The Indefensible Electoral College" cleverly points out, we are really just voting for electors who then pick the president. Perhaps we should rename it the "Electors Election," not the "Presidential Election"! Even though electors are supposed to follow their marching orders, they sometimes go rogue. If an elector has a change of heart, it could throw the entire election into chaos and spell trouble for the whole nation. That is not how democracy should run – it should reflect the people's voice, not the electors'. Some say, "If it ain't broke, don't fix it," pointing to the Electoral College's long history. But shouldn't we always strive for a better nation, and that means re-examining and tweaking our systems? Where would we be if we never challenged the British? We should always assess the Electoral College, ensuring it accurately reflects the people's choice for president.

In short, the Electoral College seems more concerned with electoral might than the people's true desires. Some states wield more power than others, and electors are granted excessive influence. If we aspire to a government that truly represents and values every citizen, we must address the Electoral College. Our goal is to ensure it mirrors the people's will, paving the way for progress as a nation.

Okay, seriously, what is up with the Electoral College? Ever stop to think about where your vote actually ends up? It doesn't directly elect the President, no sir. Instead, we pick electors who *should* vote for the President. But can we honestly count on them to do what's right for everyone?

Honestly, the Electoral College feels rigged. Some voters get a louder voice than others. Sure, these electors might seem like the smarty-pants, capable of deep thinking. Okay, that's understandable. But can we *really* trust them to make such a huge call for voters who can think perfectly well themselves? The whole thing just feels off. The winner-takes-all bit? Candidates completely ignore states they figure they'll lose anyway and zero in on those "swing" states. So why stick with a system that so many people hate? It leaves people feeling like their vote doesn't matter one bit. Sure, the Electoral College *might* think they know best, and okay, I get that, but shouldn't *we* be the ones to choose the President? "...candidates don't spend time in states they know they have no chance of winning..." How is that remotely okay? We deserve to pick our own leader, plain and simple.

And think about this, too. How are we even supposed to know who to vote for? We *can* and *should* be careful when voting for electors, and voting is crucial. You gotta vote. But we don't always have all the facts, do we? "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the two thousand fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century the system allows for much worse. Consider the state legislatures are technically responsible for picking electors , and that those electors could always defy the will of the people." Voters screw up sometimes, but not always! We should vote smart, sure, but the Electoral College shouldn't be able to just overrule us. There are different views and different electoral parties, but don't forget the 2000 election mess. "Consider the state legislatures are technically responsible for picking electors, and that those electors could always defy the will of the people." We must vote for our President, but also be mindful of who we're voting for, to avoid problems.

So, yeah, I hope you're thinking about all this. It's tough to just hand over something this important to someone else. We need to trust ourselves and make choices based on good information.

Picture this: It's Friday, 5 PM, and you're done with work! No insane traffic or loud honking—just a relaxing trip back home. Pretty cool, huh? Well, for lots of Europeans, it's just a normal Tuesday. This proves how great things can be when people don't depend so much on their cars.

Major benefit: you save dough! Having a car can really drain your wallet. Accidents and speeding fines happen, and before you know it, you're broke quicker than you can say "insurance premium." What's more, getting rid of your car means no more gas expenses. Sure, gas prices might be fine at the moment, but they always increase. If gas gets too expensive, you might avoid fixing your car, which could make things worse in the future. A bus ride could be cheap, but driving could cost you six bucks just for gas! Or, how about riding a bike? It's way cheaper to maintain a bike than a whole car.

And don't forget pollution. Cars create a huge amount of noise, light, and air pollution. Whether you realize it or not, it affects the environment. The U.S. might not be the biggest polluter right now, but more cars could change that. Perhaps totally ditching cars is impossible, but what about some limits? For example, only driving on certain days based on your license plate, or carpooling with colleagues? Even small changes can make a difference.

The truth is: People in cities with fewer cars are more content! Less stress thanks to no traffic jams or minor accidents. Seriously, driving can be a hassle, especially with all the distractions around. These distractions can cause accidents or just make you really anxious. Heidrun Walter, a mother of two, explained it best: "Having a car always made me stressed. Now I feel much happier!" She loved the lively streets, where bike sounds and kids' laughter were louder than cars. Imagine having everything you need nearby, so you can simply relax and walk or bike with your children. Sounds good, doesn't it?

So, how would using fewer cars change your life? Could it improve your health and happiness? Worth considering, isn't it? What if reducing car use made things much better?

Every day, people get in their cars to go to work, run errands, or travel. They usually don't think about the greenhouse gasses hurting our world. This pollution causes a big problem, mostly in cities. That's why countries and groups have started projects to get people to drive less. These actions have really helped to cut down on bad emissions and make things better overall!

The best part about driving less is that it helps reduce pollution and those awful greenhouse gasses. If people drive less, they can save money on gas and help the planet. In Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," she points out that cars add a lot to greenhouse gasses. In Europe, cars cause 12% of these gasses, but in the US, it's a whopping 50%! These numbers show just how much cars affect gas emissions. Just think about how much better it would be if many cities had fewer cars. The way we choose the president isn't simply about tallying the votes. A fairer system would directly count each vote. It's pretty clear the current setup is outdated.
