Ditching cars? Sounds wild, right? But loads of people are doing it. The reason? They're chasing better health and a happier vibe. Heidrun Walter, featured in Elisabeth Rosenthal's story about a German town, put it this way: "Driving? Total stress. Now? I feel amazing." Like many Vauban, Germany residents, she's found a calmer life without a car. The town? No driveways, no garages. It's a push to leave the car behind. Instead, buses, bikes, and walking are the go-to's. Forget car stress, Vauban sees owning a car as the real hassle. Over half bailed on their cars to move there and don't even miss them. Andrew Selsky, in "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," shares Carlos Arturo Plaza's words. Biking with his wife, he said, "It's so relaxing!"

Plaza also pointed out, "Cleaner air is a nice bonus." Finally, folks are getting that cars = lots of pollution. Paris, famous for smog, has been on a mission to clean its air. Robert Duffer, in "After days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city," explained how cars with even-numbered plates were banned some days, odd-numbered plates on others. The impact? Huge. Duffer noted, "Traffic? Down 60 percent in Paris after five smoggy days." Elizabeth Rosenthal, in "The End of Car Culture," thinks, "If this keeps up...Earth wins, since cars are big polluters in the USA." We Americans use a ton of stuff, which means lots of pollution. We crave the latest gadgets, but it costs more than money. Greenhouse gases are piling up, and something's gotta give. Rosenthal highlights that "President Obama set some big goals to cut America's greenhouse gas emissions last week." In car-free zones, the perks go beyond just clean air. Andrew Selsky wrote about "the construction of 118 miles of bicycle paths" and "rough sidewalks swapped for smooth ones" in Bogota, Colombia. This makes walking way more appealing. The author mentions that "officials from other countries came to Bogota to see carfree day and were pumped," including the mayor of Paraguay's capital. Seeing the good stuff makes people want to jump on board. Columbia's event has sparked talks nearby, with "two other Colombian cities, Calli and Valledupar, joined in." But car companies? They're evolving. Rosenthal explains, "But it could hurt the car business. So, companies like Ford and Mercedes are calling themselves "mobility" companies that do more than just make cars." As walking, biking, and buses gain popularity, car companies are brainstorming to keep customers, even without cars. She also shares Bill Ford's vision of car dealers "work with phone companies to build cities where 'walking, bikes, cars, and buses all work together to save time, use less stuff, pollute less, and be safer.'" It's about blending transportation with tech for the win.

Change is in the air, no doubt. Cities are experimenting with new ways to get around or sprucing up the old ones. Henry Ford might be bummed cars are losing their shine, but Mother Nature would be thrilled with our eco-efforts. The future's a mystery, but we've got one Earth, so let's treat it right.

Folks are really wanting to ditch the Electoral College, and it brings back memories of the 2000 election problems. A lot of people think it's not right, a waste of time, and needs to go.

But hold on a sec, the Electoral College does have some good stuff. Richard A. Posner wrote that it "stops elections where no one gets over half the votes." It makes sure we have a clear winner, and stops messy runoffs that can cause chaos. Posner also says the winner usually gets more Electoral College votes than the popular vote. This lowers the chance of big fights about the result if we just counted individual votes. But, the big idea is still that the people should get to choose their president. So, lots of people think the Electoral College is bad and confusing, and it has to go.

A big fear is that the popular vote winner might lose. This might mean America doesn't get the leader it really wants. Bradford Plumer says the Electoral College means "the popular vote winner could lose again." Taking away people's power to pick their leader goes against freedom. Plumer also says voters can't always "control who their electors vote for," which makes people have less say in electoral votes. This makes popular opinion matter less. So, we should get rid of the Electoral College ASAP.

Another issue is how much power electors have. Plumer says "electors could always go against what the people want," and that shows how much they affect who becomes president. That power should be with the public. He wrote that electors "have sometimes voted against their party and picked whoever they wanted," which shows they can just change their minds. The popular vote should decide everything. What happens to America should be based on what people want, and they should pick their president.

In the end, the Electoral College is not fair to Americans. We should get to pick our leader. The popular vote should win, and we should be done with the Electoral College for good.

Every single day, many of us jump into our cars to travel to our jobs or schools. Guess what though? Things are starting to change drastically! There is a rising trend to drive our vehicles less, and it's a fantastic move. Not only is it beneficial for our planet, but it also can help us feel significantly more relaxed.

If we choose to drive less, we will have fewer car fumes. That's absolutely amazing for the environment! The exhaust from cars creates that nasty smog, trapping unwanted particles in the air. Cities end up becoming super smoggy because of the exhaust. For instance, Paris once experienced a whopping 147 micrograms of junk floating in the air. After they stopped some of the cars from driving around, the smog vanished very fast – in just one day, and they didn't have to do it again! Cars can create a TON of greenhouse gasses: 12% of the greenhouse gasses are created in Europe, and a whopping 50% of the gasses are created in the US. That is how much cars impact our environment negatively. Even just one car-free day a week can seriously cut down on those gasses released in the air. Americans are even starting to change their habits! Fewer people are buying vehicles and driving around. Since '95, the number of miles each person is driving is dropping by almost 9%. If we continue to ditch the cars, we could significantly reduce gas emissions and greatly improve the environment.

Moreover, skipping the daily drive to work can greatly reduce stress. In Vauban, Germany, many people don't even own cars – only 30% of people own cars. Even though some people think you NEED a minivan for kids, Heidrun Walter prefers being car-free and doing without a car. She has stated in the past that, "When I had a car, I felt very stressed. I am much happier and relaxed now." For three consecutive years, Bogota, Colombia, has had a "Day Without Cars." The only people that are allowed to drive are taxis and busses. Even when it rains and is dark out, people love and enjoy the car-free day. Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza has also said that, "It's a great way to chill out and clean up the air for everyone." Now two other Columbian cities want to start the same tradition. And guess what? Technology now lets us connect with our friends, work right from our own home, and lets us skip the dreaded commute. That can be a HUGE stress reliever!

We used to think that cars were the very best way to get around town. We didn't think about or consider how they harmed the planet or our own mental health and happiness. Now, we should rethink our habits and see a much bigger picture.

Oh, the Electoral College? Really? It is such an awful way to select the president. It actually screws over voters because state electors will sometimes do the opposite of what people want. We do not want another Al Gore episode. To win the most votes but still lose? No thanks. This older system? Completely behind the times.

When casting a vote, you’re actually choosing "electors." The electors are expected to vote for the victor, but they are free to do as they want. Someone could gain the popular vote and still get defeated. How could this be? They don’t have enough electoral votes from each state. That’s completely unfair. Whoever everyone wants should simply win, end of story. Back in the day, it might have been that electors had to be smart to pick for the dummies. But today? Almost anyone can pick a good leader. Al Gore lost back in 2000, even though he got the most votes, and it shows how poor of a system it is.

So, what’s so bad about the Electoral College? It’s all or nothing, so to speak. You get a state, even by only a hair, and you get ALL the electoral votes. Therefore, candidates will ignore the smaller states, or states that they know they will win, and focus on big, swing states. Such as Ohio. Known for changing its mind. It may seem like a smart move, but back in 2000, 17 states were ignored. Voters there were forgotten and had no idea what was going on. During close races, almost half the voters in some states don’t even count. In California, nearly half of the 35 million voters don’t matter at all! Elections should not depend on Ohio while everyone else is ignored.

We need a way better system. Only a national popular vote can guarantee that the most popular person actually wins. Candidates would have to actually listen to everyone, even voters in rural areas! American citizens should be able to have a fair election that makes most people happy with the outcome.

Right now, in the U.S., the way presidents are elected is through the Electoral College. The people who started the country made it. Voters pick electors. These electors then vote for the president and VP. Then, Congress counts up all the votes. People who like the system think it makes sure there is a clear winner. But others don't like it so much. They say it helps big states too much, can make weird things happen, and that the winner-take-all idea is not fair.

Every state has a number of electors. It depends on how many representatives they have in Congress. So, bigger states get more electors. For example, tiny Hawaii only gets four electors. But huge Texas gets thirty-eight! Because of this, candidates spend most of their time in the places where lots of people live. As Bradford Plumer said, little Wyoming, "only has one vote. The one person from Wyoming who represents 500,000 voters has the same power as 55 people from California who represent 35 million voters. Many people vote for one party for president and another for congress. So, what the House picks might not be what the people really want."

Things can get messy with how we pick presidents right now. In 2000, Gore got more votes from people, but he still lost. Also, can we really trust electors to do what they said they would? If an elector changes their vote, it could change who wins. There have been other strange things too, such as Hawaii having two sets of electors in 1960.

The winner-take-all part of the Electoral College is a big problem. If you win the most votes in a state, you get all of its electors. Because of this, candidates don't care about states they can't win. They spend all their time on "swing states." Richard A. Posner explained that "In 2000, 17 states never even saw the candidates, including Rhode Island and South Carolina. Voters in 25 of the biggest places where ads are shown didn't see a single ad."

So, the Electoral College is not perfect. It makes a lot of problems and is not fair to voters. Even people like Nixon and Carter wanted to get rid of it. Candidates spend too much time in certain states, electors might not do what they promise, and the winner-take-all system is unfair. The Electoral College makes things worse.

Cars really make a lot of pollution. Robert Duffer points out that in France, "Diesels are 67 percent of cars..." (Duffer 16). Why? The government taxes diesel less. Texting and social media help people stay connected, meaning less trouble.

In conclusion: cars cause many problems like accidents, terrible traffic, and bad pollution. If we drive less, we can fix these problems and create a safer, healthier place for everyone.

The U.S. votes every four years, always on that Tuesday after the first Monday in November. But it's Election Day! It's not a simple pick for President, no. It's a maze! "What Is the Electoral College?" explains when you vote for someone, you're supporting their electors. Anyone can be an elector, unless they hold public office, and their choice is final. So, this isn't straightforward. "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" says over 60% want easier voting. That tells you most folks want simple voting.

Electoral votes aren't shared out equally. Big states, like California, get way more electoral votes than little Vermont. While the House tries to give everyone a voice, the Electoral College doesn't. It's all uneven. This clash is not in line with the fair country that we are.

The Electoral College confuses folks. Voters might not know who they back, and they can't control what electors do. It sucks when electors go against what most voters want. "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" says voters pick electors who then pick the President, so it's not a direct choice.

Some say getting rid of the Electoral College could be bad and un-American. "In defense of the Electoral College..." says parties pick electors who should back their person, but the popular vote winner might still lose. So, even if you vote, your choice might not win. It can stop extra elections and helps if no one gets the most votes, but it has issues.

All in all, the Electoral College hurts our country. It's hard to follow and a bit shady. It might have a few pluses, but the minuses are a lot worse. It's unequal and could mess up the country, so we should trash it for a fairer America.

Okay, so cutting back on driving can really help, yeah? One mom, Heidrun Walter, said she felt way calmer when she didn't have to drive all the time. And get this, parking can be super expensive! Can you imagine spending $40,000 just to park at your own place? Also, cars make a lot of pollution, like 12% of greenhouse gases in Europe, and even more in the US – sometimes up to half! If we drove less, we could have neighborhoods where you can walk everywhere. Also, fewer cars would mean cleaner air, which is awesome for the planet. Hybrid cars are getting popular because they're cleaner than old cars. But remember, weather can make pollution worse; cold nights and hot days trap all the bad stuff, making the air even dirtier.

Paris was having a huge problem with smog from cars, so they made some rules to fix it. If you broke the rules, you had to pay 31 bucks, and they might even take your car if you didn't stop! This really changed things with traffic, cutting it down by about 60%! The air got better after the rules came out, so Paris let some cars back in, like cars with odd license plate numbers on Tuesdays. Carlos Arturo mentioned that biking with his wife is way less stressful, and Andrew Selsky says the air is cleaner. More and more cities are starting to like this idea. For example, Bogota, Colombia, built over 118 miles of bike paths.

The mayor of Asuncion, Paraguay, Enrique Rivera, noticed that other city leaders were visiting Bogota because they really liked the changes and thought they could do the same thing in their own cities. By 2013, Americans were driving about 9% less, like back in 1995. Part of it is because of the economy, with people having less money for new cars, and people who don't have jobs don't need to drive as much. If someone couldn't afford a car, a bike might seem like a better idea to get to work or look for a job. But, some people just might not like biking or walking. Plus, cars can cost a lot of money, with gas and insurance.

So, the good news is that Americans might not be so stuck on cars anymore. As families got bigger, people drove less in 2005. Also, cars make roads more dangerous. There are fewer young drivers (16-39 years old) now, and older people are keeping their licenses longer. Studies show that young people drove 23% less between 2001 and 2009. Bill Ford wants to team up with phone companies to create smart cities where walking, biking, cars, trucks, and buses all work together to be more efficient, save resources, make less pollution, and be safer. In Germany, some areas banned cars, and now 70% of families don't own cars, and 57% sold their cars before moving there. Doing stuff like this can help clean up our air and protect our planet.

Cars? Everywhere! So many people across the globe use them to get around these days. They really do make life simpler, making errands and trips a piece of cake. Still, even with all this convenience, sometimes we forget the bad stuff. Sure, cars are handy, but leaning on them too much can stir up more problems than they solve. Using cars too much can really mess up the environment and make us stressed, maybe without even realizing it. Because of this, we should really think hard about how much we drive.

The world around us gives us what we need, like food and air, but cars pose a big problem. They pump out carbon dioxide, which dirties the air. In Robert Duff's article, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," Paris had such bad air pollution that they had to stop people from driving. This was because everyone was using diesel cars, thanks to tax breaks. Duff points out that "diesels make up 67 percent of cars in France, compared to a.3 percent on average in other parts of Western Europe, says Reuters." Plus, Duff shows that cities with lots of people usually have worse air. Because cars hurt the environment, people are trying to use them less. Elisabeth Rosenthal's "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars" tells the story of Vauban, Germany, where folks don't own cars. In Vauban, 70 percent of families don't have cars, and 57 percent sell their cars when they move there. You can still own a car, but parking is super expensive – around 40,000. People choose to walk or bike instead. Vauban shows us that it's possible to cut down on car pollution.

Besides messing up the environment, cars can sneakily cause stress. Heidrun Walter, in "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars," says she's much happier without a car: "When I had a car, I was always stressed. I'm much happier now." Also, businessman Carlos Arturo said that a car-free day in Bogota was "a great chance to lower stress and clean up the air." Cars can take over our lives, and we might not see the effects until we start using them less.

Folks just ain't got the car fever they used to have. Back in the day, a car was just a set of wheels to get you places. But nowadays, with all this whiz-bang tech, fewer and fewer people are driving, especially in cities. Now, you might think less driving is a bad sign, but hold on a sec – it's actually pretty darn good in a bunch of ways!

America's always been a car-crazy nation. We birthed the Model T, and who can forget "Mustang Sally?" Heck, even President Obama wanted us to pump the brakes on driving so much to help clean up the air. In "The End of Car Culture," he noted that things were changing big time. Turns out, studies show people are buying fewer cars, driving less, and not even bothering to get their driver's licenses as much. By 2013, driving was down almost 9% compared to the old days. What's more, less youngsters are even getting their license. It's all thanks to the internet, which lets us work in our PJs, plus cities are getting better, and we got handy phone apps for carpooling. It's not that cars are suddenly useless, but life's just easier than it was back in the sixties, thanks to tech. But hey, in the U.S., driving's more of a choice, but places like Paris, France, don't let you drive without some guidelines.

Paris, France, is a real looker of a city, no doubt. But it's got a serious smog problem. The news blared, "Paris bans driving due to smog!" They stopped some folks from driving to help clear the air. If you drive when you're not supposed to, you gotta cough up 22 euros. They only let cars with certain license plates drive on certain days. The article even said that after they did this, traffic went down 60%! Some cars could still cruise around, like hybrids and those with three or more people inside. Diesel cars, which are super popular in France, were the culprits behind most of the pollution. Regular gas cars weren't quite as bad. Just like Paris, Bogota, Colombia is trying to clean up their act.

Colombia has a car-free day – zip, zero, nada driving allowed! It's a 24-hour ban, even stricter than Paris' rules. Drive, and you'll pay 25. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" mentioned that "millions of Colombians hike, bike, skate, or take buses to work during the carfree day." Like Paris, Bogota wants folks to find other ways to get around and kick the smog habit. A businessman named Carlos Arturo Plaza said it's "a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." Driving can be a real pain, especially with road rage, which can ruin your day. Driving's alright sometimes, but there are other ways to do it. Colombia wants to "promote alternative transportation and reduce smog." Cars are getting fancier, sure, but people aren't as gaga over them anymore, even though they used to be the cat's meow. Now, they're mostly for getting from point A to point B when you don't have other options.

One significant advantage of reducing car usage? Undeniably, it's cleaner air! Urban areas grapple with severe pollution stemming from vehicles, particularly in densely congested zones. Consider Paris—news reports even indicated occasional driving bans due to alarmingly poor air quality. This pollution not only exacerbates climate issues but also harms our respiratory health. Following a period of intense smog, Paris restricted car traffic, resulting in a remarkable 60 percent reduction in traffic congestion. Temperature fluctuations can worsen pollution; cold nights coupled with warm days can trigger severe spikes.

Additionally, shunning cars fosters increased physical activity! Individuals tend to walk or bike more frequently, which is beneficial exercise. Furthermore, opportunities arise for social interaction and outdoor conversations. As pedestrian traffic increased, gyms and parks experienced higher volumes of activity, leading to the emergence of new retail outlets and eateries that stimulated local economies.

Increasingly, individuals are embracing alternative transportation options such as buses, bicycles, or walking. Research indicates a notable decline in car usage among younger demographics—a substantial 23 percent decrease from 2001 to 2009. Increased adoption of ride-sharing initiatives and alternative modes of transport would invariably lead to reduced pollution levels on a global scale. Diminished vehicle emissions would, without a doubt, enhance air quality and alleviate respiratory ailments.

The resources allocated to road maintenance and traffic infrastructure could be redirected towards more productive investments if car dependency were reduced. While car usage is widespread, alternative transportation solutions are always available. Diminished car usage also generates cost savings, particularly given the volatile nature of gasoline prices.

In essence, there exist numerous compelling arguments for minimizing or eliminating car usage. Beyond the environmental benefits of cleaner air, it is also advantageous for personal well-being. Embrace walking, biking, or public transportation for affordable mobility. Moreover, savings can be realized on fuel expenses, vehicle maintenance, and car payments.

Americans spend quite a bit of time driving. What they don't consider is alternative forms of transit. What makes finding new forms of travel amazing? Quitting a car habit has major advantages.

Imagine if we didn't use cars. Shops would be closer to home, right? Instead of malls far away, imagine stores a short walk away. It would lead to buildings, stores, and homes that are within walking distance.

Walking to stores is pretty awesome! It reduces the need to drive, plain and simple. If we all drove less, we'd walk and bike more, which is beneficial for our health. Studies even show daily walks are great for your mind.

Less driving means cleaner air, of course. Cars give off pollution that turns into smog, which is bad. Driving less helps reduce stress and makes the air cleaner.

Cars are large sources of greenhouse gases. In Europe, it's 12% of greenhouse gases. In the US, it's more like 50%! So driving less really helps the planet.

Driving isn't the only option! We can try other ways of moving that are good for us and the planet. Since there are many benefits to less driving, why not try it to improve our lives?

Imagine a world without any cars at all. It sounds impossible, but these ideas are becoming reality! For instance, some brand-new towns are being built, some cities are limiting car access, and some countries are holding car-free days. People tend to forget that cars cause a lot of pollution. We should pay attention to the damage and take action!

Vauban, Germany, is a great example. If you live there, you have to ditch your car or pay a huge $40,000 parking fee! Guess what? A report shows that "70% of families in Vauban don't own cars, and 57% sold theirs to move there." One mom, Heidrun Walter, said, "I was always stressed with a car. I'm much happier now!" This is "Smart Planning" – designing areas so people don't need cars. Cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases and a massive 50% in the U.S. Smart Planning can lower these gases and clean our air!

Paris, France, shows how bad car pollution can get. One source said, "After days of heavy pollution, Paris restricted some cars to clean the air." This cut traffic by 60% in just five days. Diesel fuel, used by 67% of French vehicles, was the main cause of smog. The weather helped, as "cold nights and warm days trapped car emissions," according to the report.

It's not just Europe. Bogota, Colombia, had its third "Day Without Cars," only allowing buses and taxis. Carlos Arturo Plaza said, "It's a good chance to reduce stress and pollution." Also, Cali and Valledupar, two other Colombian cities, had their first Day Without Cars this year!

Car use is changing even in the U.S. Believe it or not, miles driven peaked in 2005 and have been decreasing since. A source says, "In April 2013, miles driven per person were almost 9% lower than the peak, like going back to January 1995." Less car use is good for the environment but bad for car companies. Ford and Mercedes are becoming "mobility" companies. New York City has bike-sharing and new bike paths. Tunnel tolls also show this shift.

Cars have harmed our planet, and we must fix it. With these awesome, eco-friendly ideas, we're moving toward a future where cars don't control us.

The Electoral College is definitely confusing. It has been around since almost the beginning, but the reasons it was created don't really make sense anymore. Back then, most people didn't know much about politics, so it was hard for them to make good choices when voting. But now, everyone has access to news and information about candidates. Shouldn't we just pick the president with a popular vote? The president should answer to all of us, not just a few people in power.

Even if you like the Electoral College, it has some problems. When we vote for president, we're really voting for electors, and each state chooses them in their own way. These electors promise to vote for a certain candidate, and then they actually vote. But as Bradford Plumer says, you can't really control what electors do! He also says that voters get confused about electors, which can lead to people voting wrong. Sometimes, these "faithless electors" even go back on their word.

Candidates only care about "swing states." These are the states where either party could win, so they're important. Because the Electoral College gives all of a state's votes to the winner, a Democrat in a state that always votes Republican might not bother voting, since their vote probably won't matter. So, campaigns only focus on swing states. If we had a popular vote, campaigns would have to talk to everyone.

Choosing the president by popular vote would be more fair. Even smaller groups in states that always vote the same way would have a voice. A poll showed that most voters would rather pick the president directly. Even someone who likes the Electoral College admits that it could lead to a tie. They also say that one vote doesn't matter, which is crazy! That kind of thinking would make people not want to vote and would hurt our elections. Every vote matters, and everyone deserves to be represented. A popular vote is the best way to elect a president who represents all Americans. Everyone should get involved. This should apply to everyone, so let's all make sure we vote!

Following the Second World War, cars became super important. Even though some people in sources 1 and 4 are driving less, sources 2 and 3 look at cities where cars can’t drive some days! If we don’t drive as much, cities would be less polluted, and it would be easier to move around.

Air pollution is a huge issue. In source 1, Rosenthal said, "cars create 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases... and up to 50% in some U.S. places P5." A lot of cars lead to smog in cities. For example, when the Beijing Olympics happened in 2009, there were fewer cars and the air was way better! This was important to protect the athletes and tourists from getting sick, since everyone in Beijing already wore masks. Restricting car use isn't easy, but it stops bad things from happening. According to Duffer in source 2, Paris is a big city that has way less pollution than before P19. They even stopped certain cars with specific license plates from driving. If we stop polluting with cars, the air will be cleaner and the views will be better!

Even though they've grown up around cars, some younger people are not getting licenses. Selsky said that if there are fewer cars, there will be more parks, sports fields, shops, and walking areas P28. Plus, new businesses and restaurants can open! Instead of malls near highways, shopping areas are appearing near public transport or places people can walk. Rosenthal also said that his family is using public transit, carpools, and walking instead of driving cars P40. These days, it's smart to take public transit! Carpooling is useful, too. With carpools, walking, and buses, it's now easier to move around and explore the city.

Cities are starting to change to make life better for people without cars. Restricting car use has lots of benefits. Cleaner air and easier transport are making cars less important. Cutting down on car use will make life even better in the future!

So, the electoral college, right? Electors are chosen, they vote for president, and those votes are counted. But is it a good system, or is it just waiting to explode? Honestly, it should go. It's not a direct vote, and things could go south fast.

Some say the electoral college stops messy runoff elections, maybe making governing easier. Richard A. Posner pointed out that in super close races, people actually *want* runoffs. Keeping the electoral college, they argue, means fewer runoffs. But is it really a reliable solution, and what problems might it bring? Given electors pick the president, can we even trust them to prevent runoffs? Posner thinks it does remove some pressure to screw things up more. Still, this one benefit doesn't make up for all the big problems.

What happens if everything just falls apart? We're in trouble! The writer says we got lucky the 2000 election was only the worst in a century; things could definitely get worse. It's happened before. Plumer believes the electoral college has hurt the U.S. badly, and keeping it could cause even more mess. The writer reminds us that electors have voted against their parties, according to Plumer. So, it's not just useless, but also easy to mess with. It should be dumped, since it could wreck our government.

Plus, it's not a direct election at all. The Office of the Federal Register says when you vote for a candidate, you're really voting for *their* electors. So, you aren't actually choosing who runs the whole country. It just feels wrong for leaders to make big decisions affecting people's lives when they didn't vote for them. Plumer calls it unfair, old-fashioned, and just plain stupid. These concerns show how the electoral college hurts trust in government. Plumer also adds that candidates just ignore states they think they can't win, only focusing on "swing" states. Winning has become more important than what's best for the country. Are we really going to let this thing destroy us?

Basically, the electoral college? Ditch it.

Dear Florida Senator, The Founding Fathers created electors for the purpose of mirroring the public's choice for president. Nonetheless, this setup may have the outcome of the popular vote winner actually losing the election. Because the system is old-fashioned, it has a high chance of creating a tie. Many folks across the United States would instead go with true democratic ideas, yet the electoral college may undermine these ideas.

The popular vote shows what the nation really wants. A giant chunk – "over 60 percent" – wants direct presidential voting, which shows they want their votes to mean more. When the President sends troops to Afghanistan, Iraq, or Iran, people want their vote to matter, and for their voice to be heard, instead of being pushed aside by the electoral college. Just 538 electors have way more power than what millions of regular people have when they vote. Also, we have to remember that the Declaration of Independence promises rights that can't be taken away, like "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness," and liberty is closely connected to voting.

The electoral college has been around for a long time. But that's part of the problem. To keep the United States relevant and up-to-date, we have to update how constitutional rules work. The world is always changing, so the system should adapt so we improve as a whole, change, and don't create problems. Consider this as an example, "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the 2000 fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century," which brings up a possible repeat.

Even worse, the chances of a tie can make problems even bigger. As has been stated, "perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." A tie goes against what democracy is all about. A democracy should be all about the people's vote, not a legislative impasse. Source 2 claims, "the election is only a few swing voters away from catastrophe." With a tie, the popular vote is thrown to the side, which would be scary.

In short, the electoral college should be removed as it hurts U.S. people. The system makes the popular vote less important, it's outdated, and it brings the risk of a tie. Moving forward, I hope the president is chosen by the people's choice, and not by those in the capital.

Alright, imagine cars, yeah? They drive you everywhere – for tasks or hanging with friends. Now, what if they just disappeared? Strange, right? But, surprisingly, maybe some positive things could arise.

Firstly, a huge win for the environment! Cars are terrible for pollution, aren't they? Check this out: "Passenger cars? They cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases!" And get this! "Up to 50% in areas obsessed with cars in the US!" (That's from "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 5). So, say goodbye to cars, hello to cleaner air, and farewell to that gross smog. The air would simply be much better!

Also, our health would improve. Cleaner air, definitely! But also, without cars, you'd have to walk or bike around. "Vauban," according to "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 6, "with 5,500 people in a compact area, is car-free." Shops are nearby, "easy to walk to," not "miles away." So, people walk, bike, or take the bus. More exercise, less time on the couch. Nice!

Additionally, cars might be becoming less popular. "Obama's goals to reduce US emissions," states "The End of Car Culture," paragraph 29, "are supported by people buying fewer cars, driving less, and getting fewer licenses." Gas is expensive, texting while driving is dangerous, and new drivers are often clueless. It seems people are losing their love for cars. Good for the planet, right? Maybe driving will become totally irrelevant in the future.

So, fewer cars? That's a total victory! The Earth can breathe easier, and people are already giving up their cars. Why waste gas when you can walk, bike, and stay healthy? Less driving? Fantastic!

The Electoral College stirs up a lot of feelings, and has been argued about for ages. Should we get rid of it, or just leave it be? Some folks think it's totally unfair, hurting both voters and campaigns. But others believe it's the best way to pick a president. Since both sides make good points, what changes, if any, should we actually make?

The Electoral College, instead of just adding up the votes, actually tries to find a middle ground when picking a president. Each state's political parties put forward a list of electors. So, when you vote for someone, you're really backing the electors they picked.

So, why do people want to ditch the Electoral College? Well, it can get tricky. The main worry is that things could get deadlocked. How could that happen? Electors might go rogue and vote how they personally feel. You might think your vote picks electors, but they might not always do what you expect. This becomes a real problem if electors ditch their party's candidate. After Al Gore won the popular vote in 2000, but lost the election, many voters wanted direct elections. A lot of voters really hate the Electoral College.

But, some people are all for keeping it around. Each party picks electors who are likely to vote for their candidate, and usually, they do. The party will probably choose people who will back their candidate. It also shows why it's important to win over different parts of the country. A president who only wins in one small area probably isn't fit to lead everyone. They can't help everyone if they only care about one place. So, they probably won't win. Also, the Electoral College can help when no one gets a clear majority.

When we think about what to do with the Electoral College, whether to keep it or toss it, both sides have strong arguments. At the end of the day, it's up to the people to decide.

For many, cars are necessary to do things like bring children to their schools. Though, more citizens are considering that driving cars less might be a viable solution to alleviate some of the issues plaguing us.

The impact of cars on the environment is widely known. Concerns such as air pollution and greenhouse gases, which contribute to global warming, are prevalent. In Europe, approximately 12% of these harmful emissions are attributed to cars. However, in the United States, a country known for its high car usage, this figure can reach as high as 50%! Moreover, excessive traffic exacerbates these problems. As an example, in France, diesel-fueled cars have been shown to worsen urban smog, particularly during warmer months. Therefore, driving less can result in better air quality for all.

Apart from benefitting the environment, reducing car usage can have a positive impact on individuals. Throughout the world, a number of individuals have experimented with car-free living, opting for alternative modes of transportation. Heidrun Walkter, a well-off mother from Germany, gave up her car and now finds joy in walking and the sounds of bikes and children. "I was always stressed with a car. Now, I'm much happier," she stated. Additionally, reducing driving encourages people to be more physically active. In Colombia, millions participated in a car-free day, walking, biking, or using public transportation to combat smog. The initiative proved successful, fostered a sense of community, and reduced stress levels. One man, while biking with his wife, commented that it was beneficial for both the Earth and themselves.

Hence, reducing car usage offers a clear path towards improved air quality and smog reduction across the globe, while promoting the use of bikes. From Germany to France, Colombia, and the US, many countries are actively exploring strategies to mitigate the negative impacts of driving.

Senator, please consider supporting the idea of electing Presidents by popular vote. This change is crucial, and here's why. First, electors might ignore what the people want. Also, we could end up with a tie, letting the House decide. Finally, the winner-take-all way is just plain wrong.

Electors haven't always done what they were supposed to, and it could happen again. As Bradford Plumer said in "The Indefensible Electoral College," some people almost messed up the 1960 election by trying to switch electors. Sometimes, electors just vote how they want, even if it goes against their party!

The thought of a tie in the Electoral College is scary. If it happens, the House of Representatives decides, but each state only gets one vote. Think about it: Wyoming, with only 500,000 people, has the same power as California, with about 35 million! In 1976, a small change in votes in Ohio and Hawaii could have caused a tie. That was way too close!

In short, the Electoral College's winner-take-all setup isn't fair to voters. Candidates only focus on swing states, ignoring everyone else. In 2000, a huge number of states got no attention from the candidates. Many voters in big cities didn't even see one campaign ad. How can we justify letting a few voters in places like Ohio decide who becomes President? It's hard to defend.

So, Senator, let me say it again: please support a popular vote system for presidential elections. Remember that electors could go against voters, a tie would send the decision to the House, and the winner-take-all system is clearly unfair and not right for our country.
